background_cases: The cosmological argument infers the existence of God from the existence of the universe: the universe exists; whatever exists has a cause; therefore the universe has a cause, which is God.

The teleological argument infers the existence of God from the apparent design of natural organisms: organisms are adapted to their environments as if designed; design implies a designer; therefore a designer exists.

The moral argument infers the existence of God from the objectivity of moral obligations: moral obligations are objectively binding; they require a transcendent ground; therefore God exists as that ground.
definition: An argument for God's existence is compelling if and only if (i) it proceeds from uncontroversial empirical premises, (ii) the inference to a divine cause is the only plausible explanation, and (iii) the conclusion specifies the attributes of the God whose existence is proved.
conditions: proceeds from uncontroversial empirical premises
divine cause is only plausible explanation
conclusion specifies divine attributes
counterexample: The ontological argument proceeds not from empirical premises but from the concept of a maximally great being. Its premises are a priori, not empirical. The first condition fails entirely, yet the ontological argument has been defended by major philosophers from Anselm to Plantinga and is regarded by many as a serious contribution to natural theology.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all a posteriori arguments beginning from observed features of the world. The definition abstracted the empirical starting-point as essential, but the ontological argument shows that a purely a priori route to God's existence is available, proceeding from conceptual analysis rather than from experience.
passage:
Consider three arguments for the existence of God. The cosmological argument begins from the existence of the universe and infers a first cause. The teleological argument begins from the apparent design of organisms and infers a designer. The moral argument begins from the objectivity of moral obligation and infers a transcendent moral ground. Each proceeds from empirical observation — of the world's existence, its order, its moral structure — to the conclusion that God exists. From these cases one might define a compelling theistic argument: it proceeds from uncontroversial empirical premises, the inference to a divine cause is the only plausible explanation, and the conclusion specifies the attributes of the God whose existence is proved.

But this definition, drawn from a posteriori arguments, proves too narrow. Consider the ontological argument, first formulated by Anselm in the Proslogion. Anselm begins not from any empirical observation but from the concept of God as a being than which nothing greater can be conceived. He argues that such a being must exist in reality as well as in the understanding, for a being that exists only in the understanding is not truly the greatest conceivable. The argument proceeds entirely a priori; its premises are conceptual, not empirical. The first condition fails. Yet the ontological argument has been taken seriously by philosophers of the first rank, including Descartes, Leibniz, and in modified form Plantinga. The background cases were all a posteriori arguments; the definition took their empirical character as essential, when the ontological argument demonstrates that a purely conceptual route to the existence of God has been and continues to be vigorously pursued.

background_cases: A man suffers the loss of his livelihood through a flood that destroys his farm; the theodicist explains the suffering as a test of faith that will deepen his trust in God.
A woman endures a prolonged illness; the theodicist explains the suffering as an occasion for the development of patience and compassion.
A community is struck by famine; the theodicist explains the suffering as a call to charity and mutual aid among its members.
definition: A theodicy adequately addresses the problem of evil if and only if (i) it identifies a morally sufficient reason for each instance of suffering, (ii) the reason is one that the sufferer could in principle recognise, and (iii) the suffering is proportionate to the good it produces.
conditions: morally sufficient reason identified for each instance
reason recognisable by sufferer
suffering proportionate to good produced
counterexample: The suffering of an animal in a forest fire — a fawn, say, trapped by the flames and dying over several days — does not produce any recognisable good for the fawn, nor is there any morally sufficient reason that the fawn could in principle recognise, for the fawn is not a moral agent. The first and second conditions fail, yet the suffering of animals constitutes a vast portion of the evil in the world and poses a formidable challenge to theodicy.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved human sufferers who could, in principle, derive moral or spiritual benefit from their trials. The definition assumed that all suffering has a recognisable purpose for the sufferer, but animal suffering falls outside this framework entirely, revealing that the theodicy was built upon an anthropocentric sample.
passage:
Consider three cases of suffering and their theodical explanations. A man loses his livelihood in a flood; the theodicist says the loss tests and deepens his faith. A woman endures prolonged illness; the theodicist says the illness cultivates patience and compassion. A community suffers famine; the theodicist says the famine calls forth charity and solidarity. From these cases one might define an adequate theodicy: it identifies a morally sufficient reason for each instance of suffering, the reason is one the sufferer could in principle recognise, and the suffering is proportionate to the good it produces.

But this definition, drawn from cases of human suffering that admit of moral or spiritual interpretation, proves inadequate when extended beyond the human sphere. Consider the suffering of a fawn trapped in a forest fire. The animal lies in agony for days before dying. It is not a moral agent; it cannot develop patience, deepen its faith, or learn charity from its ordeal. There is no morally sufficient reason that the fawn could recognise, for recognition of moral reasons requires capacities the fawn does not possess. The first and second conditions fail entirely. Yet the suffering of animals is not a marginal phenomenon; it is a vast and pervasive feature of the natural world. The background cases were all anthropocentric — they involved human beings who could be imagined to benefit from their trials. The definition mistook this anthropocentric sample for the full range of suffering and constructed a theodicy that has nothing to say about the agony of creatures that lie beyond the reach of moral interpretation.

background_cases: A paralysed man rises and walks after a saint lays hands upon him; the event is instantaneous and visible to bystanders.
A spring of water appears in a barren desert at the prayer of a holy man; the event is sudden and public.
A storm ceases immediately upon the command of a prophet; the event is witnessed by all those aboard the vessel.
definition: An event is credibly miraculous if and only if (i) it is sudden and dramatic, (ii) it is witnessed by a crowd, and (iii) it admits of no natural explanation at the time of its occurrence.
conditions: sudden and dramatic
witnessed by crowd
admits of no natural explanation at time of occurrence
counterexample: The gradual and persistent incorruption of a saint's body after death — the body remaining free from decay for decades or centuries — is regarded by many religious traditions as a miracle. It is not sudden but slow, not dramatic in the theatrical sense but quietly remarkable, and it is not witnessed by a crowd at a single moment but observed over a long period by successive generations. The first and second conditions fail, yet the phenomenon is widely treated as miraculous.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instantaneous public spectacles. The definition abstracted suddenness and public witness as essential features, but many putative miracles are quiet, gradual, and observed over extended periods rather than in a single dramatic moment.
passage:
Consider three cases of miracle. A paralysed man rises and walks after a saint lays hands upon him; the event is instantaneous and witnessed by bystanders. A spring of water appears in a barren desert at the prayer of a holy man; the event is sudden and public. A storm ceases at the command of a prophet; those aboard the vessel observe the transformation. From these cases one might define a credible miracle: the event is sudden and dramatic, it is witnessed by a crowd, and it admits of no natural explanation at the time of its occurrence.

But this definition, drawn from spectacular public events, proves too restrictive. Consider the phenomenon of incorruption: the preservation of a saint's body from decomposition long after death. The body of Bernadette of Lourdes, for instance, was exhumed decades after her death and found remarkably intact. The phenomenon is not sudden — it unfolds over years and decades. It is not dramatic in any theatrical sense; the body simply lies in its reliquary, undecayed. And it is not witnessed by a crowd at a single moment; successive visitors observe it over a period of generations. The first and second conditions fail. Yet incorruption is widely treated as a miracle by the Catholic Church and other traditions. The background cases were all instantaneous public spectacles; the definition took their suddenness and public character as essential. But the miraculous, as the traditions understand it, may manifest itself quietly and gradually, disclosing itself over time rather than in a single dramatic moment.

background_cases: A man reads the philosophical arguments for God's existence, finds them persuasive, and adopts a religious faith on their basis.
A woman studies the historical evidence for the events recorded in scripture, judges it reliable, and comes to faith through this study.
A scholar examines the testimony of witnesses to miraculous events, weighs the evidence carefully, and concludes that faith is warranted.
definition: The adoption of religious faith is rational if and only if (i) it is preceded by a deliberate investigation of the evidence, (ii) the evidence is weighed by the canons of inductive reasoning, and (iii) the believer can articulate the evidential basis of their faith to others.
conditions: preceded by deliberate investigation of evidence
evidence weighed by canons of inductive reasoning
believer can articulate evidential basis
counterexample: A child raised in a devout household absorbs the faith of his parents and community without any deliberate investigation. He has never examined arguments for God's existence or weighed historical evidence for scripture. Yet his faith is sincere, shapes his conduct, and may be entirely appropriate to his stage of development. The first and second conditions fail, yet many theologians would regard such faith as rational in the sense of being fitting and well-ordered, even if it was not arrived at through deliberate inquiry.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved adult enquirers who came to faith through intellectual investigation. The definition treated this investigative path as the only rational route to faith, but faith may be rationally acquired through formation, communal practice, and the gradual absorption of a tradition, without any explicit evidential inquiry.
passage:
Consider three paths to faith. A man reads the philosophical arguments for God's existence and, finding them persuasive, adopts a religious faith on their basis. A woman studies the historical evidence for scriptural events, judges it reliable, and comes to believe. A scholar weighs the testimony of witnesses to miracles and concludes that faith is warranted. From these cases one might define rational faith: its adoption is preceded by deliberate investigation, the evidence is weighed inductively, and the believer can articulate his evidential basis to others.

But this definition, drawn from cases of adult intellectual conversion, proves too restrictive. Consider a child raised in a devout household. He attends worship with his family, learns the prayers and stories of his tradition, and comes to believe in God not through argument but through immersion in a form of life. He has never read a philosophical proof or weighed historical evidence. He cannot articulate an evidential basis for his faith in the manner of a scholar. The first and second conditions fail. Yet the child's faith is sincere, it shapes his moral development, and many theologians — Catholic, Reformed, and Orthodox alike — would regard it as an entirely fitting and rational response to his circumstances. The background cases were all instances of adult deliberation; the definition treated this investigative path as the only rational route. But faith may be acquired rationally through formation, communal worship, and the gradual internalisation of a tradition, without any antecedent investigation of the kind the definition demands.

background_cases: A cathedral consecrated by a bishop is regarded as sacred; elaborate rituals mark its dedication, and its interior is reserved for worship.
A Torah scroll, prepared according to strict halakhic rules and housed in the synagogue ark, is treated as sacred; special procedures govern its handling.
A relic of a saint, authenticated by ecclesiastical authority and enshrined in an altar, is venerated as sacred.
definition: An object acquires sacred status if and only if (i) it undergoes a formal rite of consecration, (ii) its sacred status is recognised by a hierarchical religious institution, and (iii) prohibitions against its misuse are codified in religious law.
conditions: undergoes formal rite of consecration
status recognised by hierarchical institution
prohibitions codified in religious law
counterexample: The River Ganges is regarded as sacred by hundreds of millions of Hindus. No single rite of consecration has ever been performed upon it; its sacredness is primordial and is woven into the mythology and devotional practice of the tradition. Hinduism does not possess a single hierarchical institution that certifies sacred status. Prohibitions concerning the river arise from custom and devotion rather than from any codified religious law in the Western sense. All three conditions fail, yet the Ganges is one of the most revered sacred objects in any religious tradition.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all drawn from institutional religions with formal hierarchies and codified law. The definition treated institutional recognition as essential to sacredness, but in traditions with decentralised authority and oral custom, sacredness arises from communal devotion and mythological tradition rather than from institutional certification.
passage:
Consider three sacred objects. A cathedral consecrated by a bishop is sacred; elaborate rituals mark its dedication. A Torah scroll prepared according to halakhic law is sacred; special rules govern its care. A relic of a saint, authenticated by ecclesiastical authority, is venerated as sacred. From these cases one might define the acquisition of sacred status: the object undergoes a formal rite of consecration, its status is recognised by a hierarchical religious institution, and prohibitions against misuse are codified in religious law.

But this definition, drawn from institutional religions with formal hierarchies and written codes, fails when applied to traditions of a different structure. Consider the River Ganges. For hundreds of millions of Hindus, the Ganges is supremely sacred — to bathe in its waters is to be purified, and its very name is uttered in prayer. Yet no single rite of consecration was ever performed upon the river. Its sacredness is primordial, rooted in mythology: the river descended from heaven through the matted hair of Shiva. Hinduism does not possess a single hierarchical institution that confers sacred status in the manner of the Catholic episcopacy. Prohibitions concerning the Ganges arise from custom and devotion, not from any codified religious law in the Western sense. All three conditions fail. The background cases were drawn from traditions with bishops, rabbis, and ecclesiastical courts; the definition took institutional machinery as essential. But the Ganges reveals that sacredness may be constituted by communal devotion and living mythology, without formal consecration, hierarchical recognition, or codified prohibition.

background_cases: Moses receives the Ten Commandments on Mount Sinai: God speaks, and the prophet records the divine words in a text.
Muhammad receives the Quran through the angel Gabriel: the divine message is transmitted in Arabic and committed to writing.
The prophet Isaiah hears the word of the Lord and delivers an oracle to the people of Israel, which is preserved in the prophetic book.
definition: An event counts as revelation if and only if (i) a divine message is communicated through a designated human intermediary, (ii) the message is expressed in propositional form, and (iii) the message is preserved in a written text.
conditions: divine message communicated through human intermediary
message expressed in propositional form
message preserved in written text
counterexample: The Christian doctrine of the Incarnation holds that God revealed himself not through a message delivered by a prophet but through the person of Christ. The revelation consists not in a set of propositions but in a life, a death, and a resurrection. The second condition fails: the Incarnation is not primarily propositional but personal. The third condition is also strained, for the revelation is the person of Christ, not the texts that record his words and deeds. Yet the Incarnation is regarded by Christians as the supreme act of divine revelation.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of prophetic, propositional revelation mediated by a human messenger. The definition treated the prophetic model as exhaustive, but the doctrine of the Incarnation represents a fundamentally different mode of revelation — personal rather than propositional, embodied rather than textual.
passage:
Consider three cases of revelation. Moses receives the Ten Commandments on Sinai: God speaks, and the prophet records the divine words. Muhammad receives the Quran through Gabriel: the message is delivered in Arabic and preserved in writing. Isaiah hears the word of the Lord and delivers an oracle that is recorded in the prophetic book. From these cases one might define revelation: a divine message is communicated through a designated human intermediary, the message is expressed in propositional form, and it is preserved in a written text.

But this definition, drawn from the prophetic traditions of the Abrahamic religions, proves too narrow when applied to the full range of what the traditions call revelation. Consider the Christian doctrine of the Incarnation. Christians hold that God revealed himself most fully not through a prophet delivering a verbal message but through the person of Jesus Christ — his life, his teaching, his suffering, his death, and his resurrection. The revelation is not primarily a set of propositions; it is a person. The Word became flesh, as the Fourth Gospel puts it, and dwelt among us. The second condition fails: the revelation is personal and experiential before it is propositional. The third condition is strained as well, for the written texts — the Gospels and Epistles — are records of the revelation, not the revelation itself. The background cases were all instances of prophetic, textual revelation; the definition took the prophetic model as the whole of revelation. But the Incarnation represents a fundamentally different mode — personal rather than propositional, embodied rather than written.

background_cases: The prohibition against murder is a divine command found in the Decalogue; it is clear, specific, and universally binding.
The command to honour one's parents is a divine command found in the same code; it is unambiguous and admits of ready application.
The prohibition against theft is a divine command recognised across the Abrahamic traditions; it is explicit and enforceable.
definition: A divine command is morally binding if and only if (i) the command is stated in clear propositional terms, (ii) it prescribes or prohibits a specific type of action, and (iii) compliance or non-compliance can be objectively determined.
conditions: stated in clear propositional terms
prescribes or prohibits specific action type
compliance objectively determinable
counterexample: The commandment to love one's neighbour as oneself is found in Leviticus and is endorsed by Christ as the second greatest commandment. Yet it is not stated in the form of a specific prohibition or prescription: it does not tell one what particular action to perform but establishes a disposition of the heart. Whether a person has loved his neighbour as himself cannot always be objectively determined from the outside. The second and third conditions fail, yet this commandment is regarded as the most fundamental of all divine commands.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all specific prohibitions whose observance is externally verifiable. The definition treated clarity and specificity as essential to divine commands, but the commandment of love is deliberately general, calling for a disposition rather than a discrete act, and its binding force is no less for its generality.
passage:
Consider three divine commands. The prohibition against murder is clear, specific, and universally binding. The command to honour one's parents is unambiguous and admits of ready application. The prohibition against theft is explicit and enforceable. From these cases one might define a morally binding divine command: it is stated in clear propositional terms, it prescribes or prohibits a specific type of action, and compliance can be objectively determined.

But this definition, drawn from the clear prohibitions of the Decalogue, proves inadequate when applied to the commandment that both the Jewish and Christian traditions regard as the most fundamental of all. The commandment to love one's neighbour as oneself, found in Leviticus and endorsed by Christ in the Gospels, is not stated as a specific prohibition or prescription. It does not say: do this particular thing, or refrain from that particular thing. It establishes a disposition of the heart — an orientation of the will towards the good of the other — that must be expressed differently in different circumstances. Whether a person has loved his neighbour as himself is often impossible to determine from the outside; the compliance is interior as much as exterior. The second and third conditions fail. The background cases were all specific, externally verifiable prohibitions; the definition took specificity and verifiability as essential. But the commandment of love, precisely because it is general and interior, encompasses and transcends all the specific commands, and its moral bindingness is no less for its open-ended character.

background_cases: The Platonic argument holds that the soul is immortal because it is simple and immaterial and therefore not subject to the dissolution that destroys composite bodies.
The Thomistic argument holds that the intellectual soul is immortal because its operations — abstract thought — transcend matter and therefore its existence does not depend on the body.
The Cartesian argument holds that the soul is a thinking substance distinct from the body and therefore capable of existing without it.
definition: An argument for immortality is sound if and only if (i) it establishes the existence of an immaterial soul, (ii) it demonstrates that the soul's operations are independent of the body, and (iii) it concludes that the soul cannot be destroyed by natural processes.
conditions: establishes existence of immaterial soul
demonstrates soul's operations independent of body
concludes soul cannot be destroyed by natural processes
counterexample: The Buddhist doctrine of rebirth affirms a form of continued existence after death without positing an immaterial soul. Buddhism explicitly denies the existence of a permanent self (atman). What continues is a causal stream of consciousness — a sequence of mental events linked by causal dependence — not an enduring immaterial substance. The first condition fails entirely, yet the Buddhist tradition holds firmly to a doctrine of post-mortem continuity.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all drawn from the Western substance-dualist tradition, which identifies survival with the persistence of an immaterial soul. The definition treated substance dualism as the only possible framework for immortality, but the Buddhist tradition shows that post-mortem continuity can be conceived without any enduring soul, as the transmission of a causal process rather than the persistence of a substance.
passage:
Consider three arguments for immortality. The Platonic argument holds that the soul is immortal because it is simple and immaterial. The Thomistic argument holds that the soul's intellectual operations transcend matter. The Cartesian argument holds that the soul is a thinking substance distinct from the body. From these cases one might define a sound argument for immortality: it establishes the existence of an immaterial soul, demonstrates that the soul's operations are independent of the body, and concludes that the soul cannot be destroyed by natural processes.

But this definition, drawn from the Western substance-dualist tradition, proves parochial when applied beyond its cultural context. Consider the Buddhist doctrine of rebirth. Buddhism explicitly denies the existence of a permanent, immaterial self — the doctrine of anatta (no-self) is one of the three marks of existence. Yet Buddhism affirms continued existence after death. What continues is not an enduring soul-substance but a causal stream of consciousness: each mental event gives rise to the next in a chain of dependent origination that spans lifetimes. There is no immaterial soul to establish, no soul whose operations might be shown to be independent of the body. The first condition of the definition fails entirely. Yet the Buddhist tradition maintains a robust doctrine of post-mortem continuity that has shaped the lives of hundreds of millions. The background cases were all substance-dualist arguments from the Western tradition; the definition took the immaterial soul as the only vehicle of survival, when the Buddhist tradition conceives survival as the continuation of a causal process without any enduring substance.

background_cases: A Christian mystic in prayer suddenly perceives a brilliant light and hears a voice that he identifies as Christ; the experience is vivid, brief, and overwhelming.
A Sufi practitioner in the midst of ecstatic chanting experiences a dissolution of the self and a sense of unity with the divine; the experience is intense and transient.
A Jewish mystic in deep meditation perceives the divine chariot described in Ezekiel; the vision is detailed, vivid, and of short duration.
definition: A religious experience is veridical if and only if (i) it occurs during an altered state of consciousness, (ii) the experience is of brief duration, and (iii) the subject reports sensory content — visual, auditory, or tactile — that they attribute to a divine source.
conditions: occurs during altered state of consciousness
of brief duration
subject reports sensory content attributed to divine source
counterexample: Brother Lawrence of the Resurrection, a seventeenth-century Carmelite lay brother, described his experience of the presence of God not as a brief ecstatic episode but as a continuous, habitual awareness maintained during the most ordinary activities — washing dishes, mending shoes, cooking in the kitchen. There was no altered state, no brief rapture, and no dramatic sensory content. All three conditions fail, yet Brother Lawrence's account of the practice of the presence of God is one of the most widely read descriptions of religious experience in the Christian tradition.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all dramatic, transient, ecstatic episodes involving vivid sensory imagery. The definition treated these features as essential, but the contemplative tradition includes a form of religious experience that is quiet, continuous, and non-sensory — an abiding awareness of the divine presence woven into the fabric of daily life.
passage:
Consider three religious experiences. A Christian mystic perceives a brilliant light and hears the voice of Christ; the experience is vivid, brief, and overwhelming. A Sufi practitioner in ecstatic chanting experiences dissolution of the self and union with the divine; the episode is intense and transient. A Jewish mystic in meditation perceives the divine chariot of Ezekiel; the vision is detailed and of short duration. From these cases one might define veridical religious experience: it occurs during an altered state of consciousness, it is of brief duration, and the subject reports sensory content attributed to a divine source.

But this definition, drawn from ecstatic peak experiences, proves too narrow. Consider Brother Lawrence of the Resurrection, a Carmelite lay brother of the seventeenth century. His spiritual classic, The Practice of the Presence of God, describes not a brief ecstatic episode but a continuous, habitual awareness of God maintained during the most ordinary activities of monastic life — peeling vegetables, scrubbing pots, sweeping the floor. There is no altered state of consciousness; Brother Lawrence is fully engaged in his mundane tasks. There is no transient rapture; the experience persists throughout the day. There is no dramatic sensory content — no visions, no voices, no lights. All three conditions fail. Yet Brother Lawrence's account has been cherished by Christians for centuries as a genuine and profound description of the experience of God's presence. The background cases were all dramatic, sensory, and transient; the definition took these features as essential, overlooking the quiet, sustained, non-sensory form of religious experience that the contemplative tradition regards as no less authentic.

background_cases: A congregation gathers in a church and recites the Lord's Prayer together; the prayer follows a fixed liturgical formula and is offered in a consecrated space.
A family sits around the dinner table and offers a grace before the meal, using a traditional form of words.
A rabbi leads the Shema in the synagogue on the Sabbath, following the prescribed order of the liturgy.
definition: An act is prayer if and only if (i) it follows a prescribed verbal formula, (ii) it is performed in a dedicated sacred space, and (iii) it is offered at a ritually appointed time.
conditions: follows prescribed verbal formula
performed in dedicated sacred space
offered at ritually appointed time
counterexample: A soldier on a battlefield, terrified and alone, cries out to God in her own spontaneous words. She is not in a sacred space — she is in a trench under fire. She follows no prescribed formula — the words tumble out unrehearsed. There is no ritually appointed time — the moment is dictated by mortal danger. All three conditions fail, yet the soldier's cry is prayer in the fullest and most urgent sense.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of formal, communal, liturgical prayer conducted in consecrated spaces at set times. The definition abstracted formality, sacred space, and ritual time as essential, but spontaneous, private prayer in extremis falls outside all three conditions while remaining unmistakably prayer.
passage:
Consider three acts of prayer. A congregation recites the Lord's Prayer in church, following a fixed liturgical formula in a consecrated space. A family offers grace before a meal, using traditional words at the appointed moment. A rabbi leads the Shema in the synagogue on the Sabbath, in accordance with the prescribed order. From these cases one might define prayer: it follows a prescribed verbal formula, it is performed in a dedicated sacred space, and it is offered at a ritually appointed time.

But this definition, drawn from formal liturgical practice, fails to accommodate the prayer that the religious traditions have often regarded as the most sincere. Consider a soldier on a battlefield. She is pinned down by enemy fire, terrified, and alone. In her extremity she cries out to God — not in the words of any prescribed formula but in the raw, unpolished language of fear and supplication. She is not in a church or a synagogue; she is in a muddy trench. There is no ritually appointed time; the moment is determined by the accident of combat. All three conditions fail. Yet the soldier's cry is prayer — perhaps the purest prayer of all, stripped of every formality and offered from the depths of the heart. The background cases were all instances of communal, formal, liturgical prayer; the definition treated their formality and setting as essential. But prayer, as the traditions themselves acknowledge, may be wholly spontaneous, entirely private, and offered in the most profane of circumstances, and its authenticity depends not upon formula, place, or time but upon the orientation of the soul towards God.

background_cases: The ontological argument proceeds from the concept of a maximally great being and, by purely a priori reasoning, concludes that such a being must exist; it requires no empirical premises.
The Cartesian proof of God's existence in the Third Meditation proceeds entirely through reflection upon the contents of the meditator's own mind, without appeal to observation of the external world.
The modal ontological argument reasons from the mere possibility of a necessary being to the conclusion that such a being exists, employing only the resources of modal logic.
definition: An argument A for the existence of God is a successful theistic proof if and only if (i) A proceeds entirely a priori, requiring no empirical premises, (ii) A is deductively valid, and (iii) A establishes the existence of a being worthy of worship.
conditions: A proceeds entirely a priori, requiring no empirical premises
A is deductively valid
A establishes the existence of a being worthy of worship
counterexample: The cosmological argument from contingency begins with the empirical observation that contingent beings exist and reasons to the conclusion that a necessary being — God — must exist as their ultimate ground. Its first premise is empirical, and its inference, whilst powerful, may be construed as abductive rather than strictly deductive. The first and second conditions fail. Yet many philosophers have regarded it as a successful theistic proof.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all a priori, purely deductive arguments — the ontological argument, the Cartesian proof, the modal argument. The definition abstracted the a priori and deductive character of these arguments as essential to a successful theistic proof. But the background cases were drawn exclusively from one family of theistic arguments, and the definition mistook the features of that family for the features of successful theistic proof as such. The cosmological argument reveals that a successful proof of God's existence may begin from empirical observation and proceed by abductive or ampliative reasoning, and that the concept of a theistic proof is broader than any definition framed upon a priori models alone can accommodate.
passage:
Consider three arguments for the existence of God that are widely regarded as serious candidates for successful theistic proofs. The ontological argument reasons from the concept of a maximally great being to the conclusion that such a being must exist, employing no empirical premises whatsoever. The Cartesian proof in the Third Meditation infers God's existence purely from reflection upon the contents of the meditator's own consciousness. The modal ontological argument moves from the bare logical possibility of a necessary being to the actuality of such a being, drawing upon nothing beyond the resources of modal logic. From these cases one might extract a definition: an argument for the existence of God is a successful theistic proof if and only if it proceeds entirely a priori, it is deductively valid, and it establishes the existence of a being worthy of worship.

But one must consider the cosmological argument from contingency, in the form developed by Leibniz and refined by subsequent philosophers. This argument begins with an empirical observation — that there exist contingent beings, beings that might not have existed — and proceeds to the conclusion that there must exist a necessary being upon which all contingent beings depend, a being that exists by the necessity of its own nature and that is, as such, a fitting object of worship and devotion. The argument has been regarded by many philosophers as among the most powerful theistic proofs available. Yet its first premise is empirical: it rests upon the observation that something exists, that the world contains contingent beings. And the inference from contingent beings to a necessary ground may be construed as abductive — an inference to the best explanation of the existence of contingent beings — rather than as a strictly deductive derivation. The first and second conditions of the definition fail. The background cases were all drawn from the family of a priori arguments, and the definition mistook the peculiar character of that family — its independence from experience, its deductive structure — for essential features of successful theistic proof. What the cosmological argument reveals is that a proof of God's existence may be rooted in the deliverances of experience and may employ modes of reasoning broader than strict deduction, and that these features do not diminish its force.

background_cases: A person endures a prolonged illness and, through the experience, develops patience, compassion, and a deeper appreciation for the preciousness of life — goods that would not have arisen without the suffering.
A community is struck by a natural disaster and responds with extraordinary acts of mutual aid and solidarity, forging bonds of communal loyalty that would not otherwise have existed.
A person suffers the loss of a close companion and, through the grief, comes to a deeper understanding of the nature of love and of the fragility of human existence.
definition: Suffering S is morally justified if and only if (i) S produces a specific, identifiable good in the sufferer or in those connected to the sufferer, (ii) the good produced could not have been achieved without S, and (iii) the good produced outweighs the suffering in magnitude and significance.
conditions: S produces a specific, identifiable good in the sufferer or in those connected to the sufferer
the good produced could not have been achieved without S
the good produced outweighs the suffering in magnitude and significance
counterexample: Consider the prolonged suffering of an infant who dies of a painful disease before reaching an age at which any good — patience, compassion, solidarity — could be developed or appreciated. No specific good is produced in the infant, who is incapable of moral or spiritual growth. The suffering does not produce goods in those connected to the infant that outweigh the infant's agony. The first and third conditions fail. Yet if the suffering of this infant is morally unjustified, the theodicist must either deny that God permits such suffering or concede that not all divinely permitted suffering is justified by goods it produces.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved sufferers or communities who were capable of moral growth and who, as a matter of fact, developed virtues or deepened bonds as a result of their suffering. The definition abstracted the soul-making pattern — suffering produces identifiable goods that outweigh it — as essential to the moral justification of suffering. But the background cases were drawn exclusively from situations involving mature agents capable of growth. The suffering of an infant who dies before any growth is possible reveals that the soul-making model has limits: not all suffering fits the pattern from which the definition was drawn, and a general account of the moral justification of suffering cannot rely solely upon the production of identifiable goods in or around the sufferer.
passage:
Consider three cases in which suffering appears to be morally justified by the goods it produces. A person endures a prolonged illness and develops, through the ordeal, virtues of patience and compassion that she would not otherwise have possessed. A community is devastated by a natural disaster and responds with acts of mutual aid that forge bonds of solidarity far deeper than any that existed before. A person loses a beloved companion and, through the grief, arrives at a profounder understanding of love and of the precariousness of human life. From these cases one might extract a definition: suffering is morally justified if and only if it produces a specific, identifiable good in the sufferer or in those connected to the sufferer, the good could not have been achieved without the suffering, and the good outweighs the suffering in magnitude and significance.

But one must consider the suffering of an infant. A child is born with a congenital disease that causes unremitting pain from birth until death at the age of six months. The infant is incapable of moral or spiritual growth; she cannot develop patience, compassion, or any of the virtues that the background cases displayed. She cannot appreciate her suffering or find meaning in it; she can only endure it. Nor does the suffering produce identifiable goods in those connected to her that could be said to outweigh the infant's agony: the parents are devastated, the family is shattered, and no bond of solidarity or deepened understanding of love emerges that could compensate for what has been endured. The first and third conditions of the definition fail. The background cases were all drawn from situations involving agents capable of growth — adults and communities that could learn, develop, and be transformed by suffering. The definition abstracted the soul-making pattern as essential to moral justification, but the infant's suffering does not fit that pattern. It reveals a limit to the soul-making model: not all suffering produces goods, and not all sufferers are capable of the growth that would render their suffering justified. An adequate account of the moral justification of suffering — if such an account is possible at all — must reckon with cases in which the soul-making model simply does not apply.

background_cases: The healing of a paralysed man who rises and walks after the pronouncement of a holy person constitutes a violation of the laws governing human physiology and is recognised as a miracle.
The transformation of water into wine at a wedding feast contravenes the known laws of chemistry and is recognised as a miracle.
The raising of a man from the dead after several days constitutes a violation of biological law and is recognised as a miracle.
definition: An event E is a miracle if and only if (i) E is a violation of a law of nature, (ii) E involves a dramatic, publicly observable physical transformation, and (iii) E is performed or announced by a recognised holy person.
conditions: E is a violation of a law of nature
E involves a dramatic, publicly observable physical transformation
E is performed or announced by a recognised holy person
counterexample: Consider a case in which a person, walking alone in the wilderness and known to no one as a holy figure, prays silently for guidance and finds that her inner disposition is wholly and instantaneously transformed — a crippling despair is replaced by a serene and unshakeable peace, in a manner that she and those who later learn of it regard as miraculous. The transformation is interior and invisible to any observer; no physical law is violated in any publicly detectable way; and the person is not recognised as a holy figure. The second and third conditions fail. Yet the event is, for those within the relevant religious tradition, a miracle — an act of God.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all dramatic, publicly observable physical transformations — healings, transmutations, resurrections — performed by recognised holy persons. The definition abstracted visibility, physicality, and association with a holy figure as essential features of miracles. But the cases were drawn from one particular genre of miracle — the spectacular, public, physical miracle of the scriptural narratives. The case of interior transformation reveals that a miracle may be private, invisible, and unassociated with any publicly recognised holy person, and that the essence of the miraculous lies in divine agency rather than in dramatic physical spectacle.
passage:
Consider three events recognised as miracles within the Western religious tradition. A paralysed man rises and walks at the word of a holy person, in violation of the laws governing human physiology. Water is transformed into wine at a wedding feast, contravening the known laws of chemistry. A dead man is raised to life after several days in the tomb, in violation of biological law. From these cases one might extract a definition: an event is a miracle if and only if it is a violation of a law of nature, it involves a dramatic, publicly observable physical transformation, and it is performed or announced by a recognised holy person. The three cases satisfy all three conditions.

But one must consider a very different sort of event. A person — not recognised as a holy figure, not known to anyone as a person of particular sanctity — is walking alone in the wilderness, burdened by a despair so profound that she has lost all desire to continue living. She prays, silently and without words, for help. And in an instant her inner disposition is wholly and utterly transformed: the despair is replaced by a serene, unshakeable peace, a sense of being held and sustained by a goodness that transcends all understanding. The transformation is complete and, by all accounts, permanent. She regards it as miraculous, and those within her religious community who later learn of it concur. Yet no publicly observable physical transformation has occurred; the change is entirely interior and invisible to any bystander. No law of nature has been violated in any publicly detectable way; the transformation is of the soul, not of the body. And the person is not a recognised holy figure; she is an ordinary woman in the grip of despair. The second and third conditions of the definition fail. The background cases were all drawn from the genre of the spectacular physical miracle, and the definition mistook the dramatic, visible, public character of such events for essential features of the miraculous. What the case of interior transformation reveals is that a miracle may be quiet, invisible, and private, and that what makes an event miraculous is not its spectacle but its origin in the agency of God.

background_cases: A theologian affirms the doctrine of the Trinity after careful study of scripture and the church fathers, assenting to the proposition that God is three persons in one substance.
A convert to Islam affirms the shahada — the declaration that there is no god but God and that Muhammad is his prophet — after a period of instruction and reflection.
A Jewish scholar affirms the oneness of God as articulated in the Shema, holding this proposition to be the foundation of all religious truth.
definition: Religious faith consists in holding that p if and only if (i) p is a doctrinal proposition affirmed by a recognised religious tradition, (ii) the subject assents to p after deliberation, and (iii) the subject can articulate the propositional content of p.
conditions: p is a doctrinal proposition affirmed by a recognised religious tradition
the subject assents to p after deliberation
the subject can articulate the propositional content of p
counterexample: Consider an illiterate peasant in a remote village who has never encountered formal doctrine and cannot articulate any propositional content, yet who lives in a state of unshakeable trust in God — a trust that permeates every aspect of her daily life, that sustains her through hardship, and that manifests itself in habitual acts of charity, patience, and gratitude. She cannot state any doctrinal proposition, she has not deliberated upon any creed, and she belongs to no recognised tradition in any formal sense. All three conditions fail. Yet her faith is genuine and profound.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of educated, articulate persons assenting to doctrinal propositions after deliberation — a theologian, a convert, a scholar. The definition abstracted doctrinal assent, deliberation, and articulability as essential to faith. But the cases were drawn from the intellectual élite of religious life. The illiterate peasant reveals that faith may be a matter of lived trust rather than propositional assent, and that the essence of faith lies not in the ability to articulate doctrines but in a fundamental orientation of the whole person towards God that may be deeper and more enduring than any intellectual formulation.
passage:
Consider three instances of religious faith. A theologian, after years of study, affirms the doctrine of the Trinity, assenting to the proposition that God is three persons in one substance. A convert to Islam, after a period of careful instruction and reflection, declares the shahada — the affirmation that there is no god but God and that Muhammad is his prophet. A Jewish scholar affirms the oneness of God as articulated in the Shema, holding this to be the most fundamental truth of his religious life. From these cases one might extract a definition: religious faith consists in holding that p, where p is a doctrinal proposition affirmed by a recognised tradition, the subject assents to p after deliberation, and the subject can articulate the propositional content of p. The three cases satisfy all three conditions.

But one must consider the illiterate peasant. In a remote village, far from any centre of theological learning, there lives a woman who has never encountered formal doctrine and who could not articulate the content of any creed. She has not deliberated upon the propositions of any recognised tradition, for she is unacquainted with them. Yet she lives in a state of trust in God so complete and so habitual that it pervades every moment of her existence. She rises each morning with a wordless gratitude; she endures hardship — and her life contains much hardship — with a patience that astonishes those who know her; she gives to the poor from her meagre resources without calculation or self-regard. Asked why she lives as she does, she says only that God is good and that she trusts him. She cannot state a doctrine; she cannot articulate a proposition; she has not deliberated upon any creed. All three conditions of the definition fail. Yet no one who has observed her life could doubt the genuineness or the depth of her faith. The background cases were drawn from the ranks of the theologically literate — persons capable of formulating and assenting to doctrinal propositions. The definition mistook the intellectual expression of faith for faith itself. What the peasant reveals is that faith may be a mode of lived trust, a fundamental orientation of the whole person towards God, that operates at a level deeper than propositional assent and that may be possessed most perfectly by those who are least able to articulate it.

background_cases: A congregation gathers in a church and recites a litany of prayers for the sick, the poor, and the bereaved, following a prescribed liturgical form.
A family gathers around a table before a meal and offers a prayer of thanksgiving, using a traditional formula handed down through generations.
A monk in a monastery chants the psalms at the appointed hours according to the rule of his order, following a fixed sequence of prayers.
definition: An act A is an act of prayer if and only if (i) A follows a prescribed verbal formula, (ii) A is performed communally or within a recognised liturgical setting, and (iii) A is directed towards a specific, named deity.
conditions: A follows a prescribed verbal formula
A is performed communally or within a recognised liturgical setting
A is directed towards a specific, named deity
counterexample: Consider a woman who, in a moment of anguish, falls silent and simply opens her heart to whatever transcendent reality may be listening — without words, without formula, alone in her room, and without directing her prayer to any specific, named deity, for she is uncertain of the nature of the divine. She would describe what she does as prayer, and the great spiritual traditions of the world would recognise it as such. All three conditions fail, yet the act is prayer.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of formal, communal, liturgical prayer directed towards a named deity — a congregation reciting a litany, a family saying grace, a monk chanting psalms. The definition abstracted formality, communal setting, and specificity of address as essential to prayer. But the cases were drawn exclusively from institutional, liturgical contexts. The woman's wordless, solitary, undirected opening of the heart reveals that prayer may be spontaneous, private, and directed towards a transcendent reality that the one who prays cannot name, and that the essence of prayer lies in the intentional turning of the self towards the divine, however unnamed and unknown that divine may be.
passage:
Consider three acts that are uncontroversially instances of prayer. A congregation gathers in a church and recites a litany of intercessions, following a prescribed liturgical form. A family assembles before a meal and offers a traditional prayer of thanksgiving. A monk chants the psalms at the canonical hours in accordance with the rule of his order. From these cases one might extract a definition: an act is an act of prayer if and only if it follows a prescribed verbal formula, it is performed communally or within a recognised liturgical setting, and it is directed towards a specific, named deity. The three cases satisfy all three conditions.

But one must consider the woman who, alone in her room at night, overcome by an anguish she cannot articulate, falls silent and simply opens her heart. She uses no words; no prescribed formula guides her. She is alone; there is no community and no liturgical setting. And she does not direct her prayer to any specific, named deity, for she is uncertain of the nature of the divine — uncertain whether there is a God, uncertain what name, if any, the transcendent reality bears. She simply opens herself, in mute need and desperate hope, to whatever may be listening. She would call what she does prayer, and the spiritual masters of many traditions would agree: this wordless, inarticulate opening of the self to the unknown divine is prayer in its most elemental form. Yet all three conditions of the definition fail. There is no formula, no communal or liturgical context, and no specific deity addressed. The background cases were institutional and formal, and the definition mistook the institutional trappings of prayer — formula, community, liturgy, named address — for the essence of prayer. What the woman's experience reveals is that prayer, at its deepest, may be the bare turning of the self towards a transcendent reality that one cannot name, in a silence that precedes and exceeds all words.

background_cases: The Kaaba in Mecca is sacred: it is an ancient structure, set apart from all profane use, associated with the founding narrative of Islam, and the focal point of the hajj pilgrimage.
The Western Wall in Jerusalem is sacred: it is a remnant of the ancient Temple, set apart from ordinary use, associated with centuries of prayer and lamentation, and a site of pilgrimage.
The Bodhi Tree in Bodh Gaya is sacred: it is an ancient tree, set apart from profane use, associated with the enlightenment of the Buddha, and a site of pilgrimage.
definition: A place P is sacred if and only if (i) P is of great antiquity, (ii) P is associated with a founding event of a major religious tradition, and (iii) P is a site of organised pilgrimage.
conditions: P is of great antiquity
P is associated with a founding event of a major religious tradition
P is a site of organised pilgrimage
counterexample: Consider a place where a devastating accident has recently occurred — a bridge, perhaps, that collapsed, killing many persons. Within days, the site becomes a place of spontaneous devotion: candles are lit, flowers are laid, prayers are offered, and those who visit report a palpable sense of the numinous — a feeling of being in the presence of something that transcends ordinary experience. The place is not of great antiquity; it became sacred within days. It is not associated with the founding event of any religious tradition. And the visits are spontaneous, not organised pilgrimage. All three conditions fail, yet the place is sacred.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all ancient sites associated with the founding events of major religions and serving as destinations for organised pilgrimage — the Kaaba, the Western Wall, the Bodhi Tree. The definition abstracted antiquity, foundational association, and organised pilgrimage as essential to sacredness. But the cases were drawn from the most established and institutionalised examples of sacred places. The site of a recent tragedy reveals that sacredness may erupt spontaneously, in places of no antiquity and no institutional significance, wherever human beings encounter the numinous — and that the essence of the sacred lies not in age or institutional association but in the irruption of a transcendent dimension into ordinary space.
passage:
Consider three places universally recognised as sacred. The Kaaba in Mecca, an ancient structure associated with the founding narrative of Islam and the focal point of the annual hajj. The Western Wall in Jerusalem, a remnant of the ancient Temple associated with centuries of Jewish prayer and pilgrimage. The Bodhi Tree in Bodh Gaya, an ancient tree associated with the Buddha's enlightenment and a site of pilgrimage for Buddhists from across the world. From these cases one might extract a definition: a place is sacred if and only if it is of great antiquity, it is associated with a founding event of a major religious tradition, and it is a site of organised pilgrimage.

But one must consider a very different case. A bridge in an ordinary town collapses without warning, killing many persons. Within hours, the site becomes a place of spontaneous devotion. People come — not as organised pilgrims, but as individuals drawn by grief, by solidarity, and by something more difficult to name. They light candles, lay flowers, stand in silence. Many report a sense of the numinous at the site — a feeling of solemnity, of being in the presence of something that transcends the ordinary. The place acquires a character that can only be described as sacred. It is treated with a reverence quite different from the respect one accords to an ordinary memorial; it becomes a place where people come to pray, to weep, to be quiet in the presence of what they feel to be holy ground. Yet the site is not ancient; it became sacred within days of the disaster. It is not associated with any founding event of any religious tradition. And the visits are spontaneous, not organised pilgrimages. All three conditions of the definition fail. The background cases were the most established, institutional, and ancient of sacred places, and the definition mistook their distinctive features for essential conditions of sacredness. What the site of the tragedy reveals is that the sacred may erupt suddenly, in places of no antiquity and no institutional significance, wherever human beings are confronted with the numinous — and that sacredness is a quality that inheres not in age or tradition but in the irruption of a transcendent dimension into the fabric of ordinary space.

background_cases: The giving of the Torah on Mount Sinai is a paradigm of divine revelation: God speaks audible words to Moses, and the content is recorded in a text that is preserved and transmitted.
The Qur'an is understood as divine revelation: the angel Jibril communicates the words of God to the Prophet Muhammad, and the content is recorded in a text of unsurpassed literary beauty.
The prophetic books of the Hebrew Bible constitute divine revelation: God communicates specific messages to named individuals, who record them in written form.
definition: A divine revelation R is authoritative if and only if (i) R is communicated through a specific, identifiable human recipient, (ii) R is preserved in a written text, and (iii) R contains specific, articulable doctrinal or moral content.
conditions: R is communicated through a specific, identifiable human recipient
R is preserved in a written text
R contains specific, articulable doctrinal or moral content
counterexample: Consider the revelation that many theologians hold to be present in the beauty and order of the natural world — what has been called general or natural revelation. On this view, the heavens declare the glory of God; the intricate order of nature reveals the wisdom and power of its creator. This revelation is not communicated through any specific human recipient; it is available to all who have eyes to see. It is not preserved in any written text; it is inscribed, as it were, in the fabric of creation itself. And its content is not specific or articulable in the manner of a doctrinal statement; it is, rather, a diffuse impression of majesty, order, and purposive intelligence. All three conditions fail. Yet natural revelation is held by many theologians to be genuinely authoritative — a real disclosure of the divine character.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of special revelation — specific communications from God to specific human recipients, preserved in sacred texts containing articulable doctrinal and moral content. The definition abstracted the features of special revelation — specificity of recipient, textual preservation, articulable content — as essential to authoritative revelation. But the cases were drawn exclusively from the domain of special revelation, overlooking the vast tradition of natural or general revelation. The beauty and order of creation reveals that divine revelation may be diffuse, universal, non-textual, and inarticulate, and that the concept of authoritative revelation is broader than any definition framed upon special revelation alone can accommodate.
passage:
Consider three instances of divine revelation that are held to be authoritative within the Abrahamic traditions. The giving of the Torah on Mount Sinai: God speaks to Moses, and the content is recorded in a sacred text. The communication of the Qur'an: the angel Jibril delivers the words of God to the Prophet Muhammad, and they are preserved in a text of unsurpassed beauty. The prophetic books of the Hebrew Bible: God communicates specific messages to named prophets, who record them in writing. From these cases one might extract a definition: a divine revelation is authoritative if and only if it is communicated through a specific, identifiable human recipient, it is preserved in a written text, and it contains specific, articulable doctrinal or moral content.

But one must consider what theologians have called general or natural revelation. Many thinkers — from Paul's letter to the Romans through the natural theology of the medieval period and beyond — have held that the beauty, order, and intricate structure of the natural world constitute a genuine revelation of the divine character. The heavens declare the glory of God; the wisdom and power of the creator are manifest in the design of creation. This revelation is not communicated through any specific human recipient; it is available to every person who contemplates the natural world with attention and humility. It is not preserved in any written text; it is inscribed in the fabric of creation itself, in the laws of nature and the beauty of the cosmos. And its content is not specific or articulable in the manner of a doctrinal proposition; it is a diffuse, pervasive impression of majesty, intelligence, and purposive order that resists reduction to any formula. All three conditions of the definition fail. Yet natural revelation is held by many theologians to be genuinely authoritative — not merely a faint suggestion of the divine, but a real and weighty disclosure of God's wisdom and power that leaves those who ignore it without excuse. The background cases were all instances of special revelation, and the definition mistook the features of special revelation for the features of authoritative revelation as such.

background_cases: God commands Abraham to leave his homeland and journey to an unknown land; the command is clear, specific, and directly communicated, and Abraham obeys.
God commands Moses to confront Pharaoh and demand the release of the Israelites; the command is unambiguous and Moses, after initial reluctance, obeys.
God commands Jonah to preach repentance to the city of Nineveh; the command is explicit and, after a period of flight and tribulation, Jonah obeys.
definition: A divine command is morally binding upon a person S if and only if (i) the command is communicated directly and unambiguously to S, (ii) S is able to identify the source of the command as God, and (iii) the content of the command is specific enough that S can determine precisely what action is required.
conditions: the command is communicated directly and unambiguously to S
S is able to identify the source of the command as God
the content of the command is specific enough that S can determine precisely what action is required
counterexample: Consider a person who feels a persistent, inarticulate moral pull towards a course of action — a growing sense that she ought to devote her life to the care of the destitute, though no voice has spoken to her, no unambiguous message has been delivered, and the pull is more a diffuse moral pressure than a specific directive. She cannot identify the source with certainty; she suspects it may be God but cannot be sure. And the content is not precise: she knows only that she is being drawn towards the poor, not what specific actions are required. Yet she comes to regard this pull as a divine command, and the theological tradition of discernment supports her interpretation. All three conditions fail.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all dramatic, narrative instances of divine command — God speaking directly to Abraham, Moses, Jonah — in which the communication was clear, the source identifiable, and the content specific. The definition abstracted directness, identifiability, and specificity as essential to a morally binding divine command. But the cases were drawn from the extraordinary narratives of scripture, not from the ordinary moral life of believers. The case of the inarticulate moral pull reveals that divine commands may be experienced as vague, diffuse, and uncertain in origin, and that what makes them binding may be something other than the clarity and specificity of their communication — perhaps the persistent, inescapable character of the moral demand they impose.
passage:
Consider three cases in which a divine command is held to be morally binding. God commands Abraham to leave his homeland and travel to an unknown land; the command is direct, unambiguous, and Abraham can identify its source as God. God commands Moses to confront Pharaoh; the command is specific and its divine origin unmistakable. God commands Jonah to preach to Nineveh; again the command is explicit, its source identifiable, and its content precise. From these cases one might extract a definition: a divine command is morally binding upon a person if and only if it is communicated directly and unambiguously, the person can identify its source as God, and its content is specific enough to determine precisely what action is required.

But one must consider a very different mode of divine command — the mode that is, arguably, far more common in the actual moral experience of religious persons. A woman feels a persistent, inarticulate moral pull towards a course of action. Over weeks and months, she becomes increasingly convinced that she ought to devote her life to the care of the destitute. No voice has spoken to her; no vision has appeared; no unambiguous message has been delivered. The pull is more a diffuse moral pressure than a specific directive — a growing sense of being summoned, of being under an obligation whose source she cannot clearly identify and whose precise content she cannot fully articulate. She suspects that the pull comes from God, but she cannot be certain; the experience lacks the dramatic clarity of the scriptural narratives. And the content is imprecise: she knows that she is being drawn towards the poor, but she does not know exactly what she is to do — where she is to go, how she is to serve, what form her devotion is to take. All three conditions of the definition fail. Yet the theological tradition of discernment — the tradition that counsels attentiveness to the quiet movements of the soul — recognises such experiences as authentic encounters with the divine will. The background cases were extraordinary, dramatic, and narratively vivid, and the definition mistook their distinctive features for essential conditions of morally binding divine command. What the woman's experience reveals is that divine command, in ordinary moral life, may be quiet, diffuse, uncertain in origin, and imprecise in content, yet morally binding nonetheless.

background_cases: Paul's experience on the road to Damascus involved a blinding light, a voice from heaven, and a sudden, total conversion from persecutor to apostle.

The visions of Julian of Norwich involved vivid sensory imagery — the sight of blood flowing from the crucifix, the feeling of being in the presence of Christ — and produced a body of mystical theology.
The experience of Moses before the burning bush involved a visible miraculous phenomenon, an audible divine voice, and a commission to lead the people of Israel out of bondage.
definition: A religious experience E is of the highest order if and only if (i) E involves vivid sensory or quasi-sensory phenomena, (ii) E produces a dramatic and sudden transformation in the subject's life, and (iii) E issues in a specific vocation or commission.
conditions: E involves vivid sensory or quasi-sensory phenomena
E produces a dramatic and sudden transformation in the subject's life
E issues in a specific vocation or commission
counterexample: Consider the experience described by certain contemplatives — a gradual, almost imperceptible deepening of awareness over many years of disciplined prayer, in which the practitioner comes to abide in a continuous, quiet consciousness of the divine presence. There are no vivid sensory phenomena; the experience is characterised by its very lack of sensory content — by silence, emptiness, and a kind of luminous darkness. There is no sudden transformation; the change is incremental, extending over decades. And no specific vocation or commission is received; the practitioner simply continues in the life of prayer, deepened but not redirected. All three conditions fail, yet the contemplative tradition holds this experience to be of the very highest order — higher, indeed, than visions and voices.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all dramatic, sudden, sensory-rich experiences issuing in specific vocations — Paul's conversion, Julian's visions, Moses's commission. The definition abstracted vivid phenomenology, sudden transformation, and specific vocation as essential to the highest order of religious experience. But the cases were drawn from the most dramatic and narratively compelling episodes in the history of religion. The contemplative's gradual deepening reveals that the highest religious experience may be characterised not by dramatic phenomena but by their absence — by silence, by the stripping away of all sensory content, and by a patient, incremental transformation that unfolds over a lifetime. The definition mistook the spectacular for the supreme, when the contemplative tradition consistently holds that the quiet, imageless awareness of God surpasses all visions and voices.
passage:
Consider three religious experiences commonly regarded as of the highest order. Paul's experience on the road to Damascus: a blinding light, a voice from heaven, an instantaneous conversion from persecutor of the church to its greatest apostle. The visions of Julian of Norwich: vivid imagery of the crucified Christ, accompanied by profound theological insight, producing a body of mystical writing of enduring significance. The experience of Moses before the burning bush: a visible miracle, an audible divine voice, and a specific commission to lead the people of Israel to freedom. From these cases one might extract a definition: a religious experience is of the highest order if and only if it involves vivid sensory or quasi-sensory phenomena, it produces a dramatic and sudden transformation in the subject's life, and it issues in a specific vocation or commission.

But one must consider the experience described by the great contemplatives — by the author of the Cloud of Unknowing, by John of the Cross, by Meister Eckhart. These writers describe a form of religious experience that is the very antithesis of the dramatic. Over many years of disciplined prayer — decades, in some cases — the contemplative comes to abide in a continuous, quiet awareness of the divine presence. This awareness is characterised not by vivid phenomena but by their absence: the contemplative has passed beyond images, beyond visions, beyond voices, into what John of the Cross calls the dark night of the soul — a state of luminous emptiness in which all sensory and intellectual content has been stripped away, leaving only the bare, wordless awareness of God. There is no sudden transformation; the change is gradual, almost imperceptible, extending over the whole of a life devoted to prayer. And no specific vocation or commission is received; the contemplative simply continues in the life of prayer, deepened and purified but not redirected. All three conditions of the definition fail. Yet the contemplative tradition, with remarkable unanimity, holds this experience to be of the very highest order — superior to visions, voices, and ecstasies, which are regarded as preliminary stages to be transcended. The background cases were spectacular and dramatic, and the definition mistook their vividness for a mark of supreme value, when in truth the highest religious experience may be characterised by silence, darkness, and the patient, lifelong work of being emptied of all that is not God.

background_cases: The revelation of the Ten Commandments to Moses on Mount Sinai, in which God communicated a set of explicit propositions — moral and liturgical commands — to a chosen recipient.

The revelation of the Qur'an to Muhammad through the angel Jibril, in which the divine message was delivered in the form of specific verbal utterances that the Prophet recited and that were subsequently recorded in writing.
The revelation to the prophet Isaiah in the Temple, in which God spoke to the prophet in words that Isaiah reported and that were preserved in the scriptural text.
definition: An event E constitutes a divine revelation if and only if (i) E consists in the communication of specific propositions from God to a human recipient, (ii) the propositions are communicated in the form of words — spoken, heard, or inscribed, (iii) the recipient is a recognised prophet or messenger chosen by God for this purpose.
conditions: E consists in the communication of specific propositions from God to a human recipient
the propositions are communicated in the form of words
the recipient is a recognised prophet or messenger chosen by God
counterexample: Consider the case of a person who, contemplating the beauty and order of the natural world — the regularity of the seasons, the intricacy of a living organism, the vastness of the night sky — comes to an overwhelming conviction that the world is the work of a benevolent creator. She has not received specific propositions from God; no words have been spoken to her. She is not a recognised prophet or messenger. Yet she regards her experience as a revelation — an unveiling of the divine character through the contemplation of creation — and theologians within several traditions would concur, holding that God reveals himself not only through words addressed to prophets but through the order and beauty of the created world.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of propositional revelation — God communicating specific verbal messages to chosen prophets. The definition abstracted the propositional, verbal, and prophetic character of these events as essential to revelation. But the cases were drawn exclusively from the tradition of prophetic, scriptural revelation, and the definition mistook the features of that particular mode for the features of revelation as such. The case of the contemplative who apprehends the divine through the beauty of nature reveals that revelation may be non-propositional, non-verbal, and available to persons who are not prophets. The definition confused one mode of revelation with revelation itself, overlooking what theologians have called general or natural revelation — the disclosure of God through the created order.
passage:
Consider three events recognised as divine revelations within the Western religious traditions. The revelation of the Ten Commandments to Moses on Mount Sinai, in which God communicated a set of specific moral and liturgical commands in the form of words spoken to a chosen recipient. The revelation of the Qur'an to Muhammad, in which the divine message was delivered through an angelic intermediary in the form of specific verbal utterances that the Prophet recited and that were subsequently preserved in writing. The revelation to Isaiah in the Temple, in which God addressed the prophet in words that Isaiah reported and that became part of the sacred scripture. From these cases one might extract a definition: an event constitutes a divine revelation if and only if it consists in the communication of specific propositions from God to a human recipient, the propositions are communicated in the form of words, and the recipient is a recognised prophet or messenger.
