But one must consider the case of a person — not a prophet, not recognised by any community as a messenger of God — who, in the course of an ordinary day, contemplates the beauty and order of the natural world and is seized by an overwhelming conviction that the world is the work of a benevolent intelligence. She watches the unfolding of a flower in the morning light, observes the intricate structure of a leaf, looks up at the stars on a clear night, and finds herself in the grip of a certainty that these things are not the products of blind chance but the expressions of a creative will. No specific propositions have been communicated to her; she has not heard words, whether spoken or inscribed. She is not a prophet; no community regards her as a messenger of God. Yet she regards her experience as a revelation — an unveiling of the divine character — and theologians within several traditions would support her. The Psalmist declared that the heavens declare the glory of God, and generations of theologians have held that God reveals himself not only through words addressed to prophets but through the order, beauty, and intelligibility of the created world. The definition, drawn from cases of propositional, verbal, prophetic revelation, mistook the features of that particular mode of divine self-disclosure for the features of revelation as such, and thereby excluded the vast domain of what has been called natural or general revelation — the disclosure of God through the created order to any person who attends to it with an open heart.

background_cases: The doctrine that the individual soul survives the death of the body and continues to exist as a conscious, personal being in a heavenly realm, retaining its memories, its character, and its identity.
The doctrine that the righteous dead shall be raised bodily at the Last Judgement, restored to physical existence in a glorified form, and shall live forever in the presence of God.
The doctrine that the disembodied soul, having been purified by a period of purgation, enters into eternal communion with God, retaining its personal identity and its capacity for thought and love.
definition: A person P achieves immortality if and only if (i) P's individual consciousness survives the death of the body, (ii) P retains personal identity — the same memories, character, and sense of self — after death, and (iii) P's post-mortem existence is everlasting and without end.
conditions: P's individual consciousness survives bodily death
P retains personal identity — memories, character, and sense of self — after death
P's post-mortem existence is everlasting and without end
counterexample: Consider the Buddhist doctrine of rebirth. According to this doctrine, when a person dies, the karmic forces generated by his actions give rise to a new existence — a new being whose character and circumstances are shaped by the karma of the previous life. This is held to be a form of immortality; the cycle of death and rebirth continues without end. Yet individual consciousness does not survive in any straightforward sense; the new being is not the same person, does not possess the same memories, and does not retain the same sense of self. Personal identity is not preserved; the Buddhist tradition explicitly denies the existence of a permanent, unchanging self that persists from one life to the next. What persists is a causal stream, not a person. Yet the doctrine is regarded, within one of the world's great religious traditions, as an account of how sentient beings persist beyond death.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all drawn from theistic traditions that affirm the survival of the individual soul — the heavenly afterlife, the bodily resurrection, the purified soul in eternal communion with God. The definition abstracted the survival of individual consciousness, the retention of personal identity, and everlasting duration as essential to immortality. But the cases were drawn from a particular family of religious traditions, and the definition mistook the features of theistic personal immortality for the features of immortality as such. The Buddhist doctrine of rebirth reveals that a religious tradition may affirm a form of post-mortem continuity that is neither the survival of individual consciousness nor the retention of personal identity, but rather the perpetuation of a causal stream in which no permanent self persists. The definition confused one conception of immortality — the personal, theistic conception — with the concept of immortality itself.
passage:
Consider three doctrines of immortality drawn from the theistic traditions. The doctrine that the individual soul survives the death of the body and continues as a conscious, personal being in a heavenly realm, retaining its memories, its character, and its sense of self. The doctrine of bodily resurrection, according to which the righteous dead shall be raised in physical form at the Last Judgement and shall live forever in the presence of God. The doctrine that the disembodied soul, after a period of purification, enters into eternal communion with the divine, retaining its personal identity and its capacities for thought and love. From these cases one might extract a definition: a person achieves immortality if and only if the person's individual consciousness survives bodily death, the person retains personal identity after death, and the post-mortem existence is everlasting.

But one must consider the Buddhist doctrine of rebirth. According to the Buddhist tradition, when a person dies, the karmic energies generated by his thoughts, words, and deeds do not perish with the body. They give rise to a new existence — a new sentient being whose character, dispositions, and circumstances are shaped by the accumulated karma of the previous life. The cycle of death and rebirth continues, life after life, age after age, until the being attains liberation. This is held, within the Buddhist tradition, to be an account of how sentient existence persists beyond death — a doctrine of post-mortem continuity that is taken with the utmost seriousness by hundreds of millions of persons. Yet individual consciousness does not survive in any straightforward sense. The new being that arises in dependence upon the karma of the deceased is not the same person; it does not possess the same memories, does not have the same sense of self, and may not even be of the same species. Personal identity is not retained; the Buddhist tradition explicitly denies the existence of a permanent, unchanging self — an atman — that transmigrates from one body to the next. What persists is a causal continuity, a stream of conditioned arising, not a person in the sense that the theistic traditions understand personhood. The definition, drawn from theistic doctrines in which the survival of the individual soul is central, mistook the features of personal, theistic immortality for the essential features of immortality as such, and thereby excluded a conception of post-mortem continuity that dispenses with the very notion of a persisting self.

background_cases: A child is born with a painful and incurable disease through no fault of its own, and suffers grievously throughout its short life; this suffering appears purposeless and undeserved.
An earthquake destroys a city, killing thousands of innocent persons who had no warning and no means of escape; the destruction appears indiscriminate and without moral justification.

A famine caused by drought kills a great number of persons in a region where the inhabitants had no means of preventing or mitigating the natural disaster; the suffering is on a vast scale and falls upon the innocent.
definition: The existence of evil is incompatible with the existence of God if and only if (i) every instance of evil is an instance of suffering that serves no purpose — that is, it does not lead to any greater good, (ii) every instance of evil is such that an omnipotent being could have prevented it without thereby preventing any good, and (iii) every instance of evil is such that an omniscient being would have known of it in advance and could have acted to prevent it.
conditions: every instance of evil is purposeless suffering that leads to no greater good
every instance of evil could have been prevented by an omnipotent being without preventing any good
every instance of evil was foreknown by an omniscient being who could have prevented it
counterexample: Consider the suffering involved in the training of a soldier for battle. The recruit endures exhaustion, pain, deprivation, and fear during her training. This suffering is real and, at the time it is endured, severe. An omnipotent being could have prevented it, and an omniscient being would have foreseen it. But the suffering serves a purpose: it prepares the soldier to defend her country and to protect the lives of her fellow citizens. The suffering leads to a greater good — the capacity for effective defence — that could not have been achieved without it. The first condition fails: the suffering is not purposeless. Yet the suffering is genuine evil — it is real pain, really endured — and the argument from evil must accommodate the possibility that some instances of genuine evil serve a purpose that justifies their occurrence.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the sick child, the earthquake, the famine — were all instances of apparently purposeless suffering inflicted upon the innocent, suffering that seemed to serve no conceivable greater good. The definition abstracted purposelessness as a feature of all evil and asserted that every instance of evil is suffering that leads to no greater good. But the background cases were drawn from the domain of the most extreme and apparently gratuitous suffering, and the definition mistook the features of that domain for the features of evil as such. The case of the soldier's training reveals that some genuine suffering does serve a purpose and does lead to a greater good, and that the argument from evil cannot proceed on the assumption that all evil is purposeless. The definition confused the most troubling species of evil — gratuitous suffering — with evil in general, and thereby rendered the argument vulnerable to the observation that at least some evil is not purposeless.
passage:
Consider three cases that press the problem of evil with particular force. A child is born with a painful and incurable disease. Through no fault of its own, it suffers grievously from the first days of its life until its early death, and no one can explain what purpose this suffering serves or what greater good it promotes. An earthquake destroys a city, killing thousands of women, women, and children who had no warning and no possibility of escape, and whose deaths appear to serve no moral or spiritual purpose whatever. A prolonged drought causes a famine that kills a great number of persons in a region where the inhabitants had no means of preventing or mitigating the disaster; the suffering is immense, and it falls upon the innocent without discrimination. From these cases one might construct a definition of the incompatibility of evil with God's existence: the existence of evil is incompatible with the existence of God if and only if every instance of evil is purposeless suffering that leads to no greater good, every instance could have been prevented by an omnipotent being without loss of any good, and every instance was foreknown by an omniscient being who could have acted.

But one must consider the suffering involved in the training of a soldier for the defence of her country. The young recruit is subjected to forced marches in foul weather, to exhaustion, to hunger, to the deliberate infliction of hardship and the exposure to fear. Her suffering is real; her body aches, her spirits flag, and there are moments when her endurance is tested to its limit. An omnipotent being could have prevented this suffering, and an omniscient being would have foreseen it. Yet the suffering serves a purpose that cannot be achieved without it. The hardships of training develop in the soldier the physical endurance, the mental fortitude, and the habits of discipline that will enable her to defend her country and to protect the lives of those who depend upon her. The suffering leads to a greater good — the capacity for effective military service — and this greater good could not have been secured by gentler means, for a soldier who has not been hardened by training is unfit for battle. The first condition of the definition fails: this suffering is not purposeless. It is genuine evil — real pain, really endured — but it serves a recognisable end. The background cases were drawn from the domain of the most extreme and apparently gratuitous suffering — sick children, earthquakes, famines — and the definition assumed that all evil shares the character of these cases. The soldier's training reveals that some evil is purposive, that some suffering leads to a greater good, and that the argument from evil cannot proceed on the blanket assumption that every instance of suffering is without purpose. The definition confused the most troubling cases of evil with evil as a whole.

background_cases: The parting of the Red Sea, as narrated in the book of Exodus, is regarded as a miracle because it was an event that defied the known behaviour of water, occurred at a moment of crisis to deliver a people from destruction, and was attributed to divine agency.

The healing of the blind man at Siloam, as narrated in the Gospel of John, is regarded as a miracle because it restored sight in a manner that defied medical possibility, occurred in a context of religious significance, and was attributed to divine power.

The liquefaction of the blood of San Gennaro, observed periodically in Naples, is regarded by many as a miracle because it involves a physical transformation that defies the normal behaviour of dried blood, occurs at a religiously significant moment, and is attributed to the saint's intercession.
definition: An event E is a miracle if and only if (i) E involves a physical occurrence that is incompatible with the known laws of nature, (ii) E occurs in a context of religious significance — that is, in association with a sacred person, place, or occasion, and (iii) E is witnessed by multiple observers whose testimony is consistent.
conditions: E involves a physical occurrence incompatible with known natural laws
E occurs in a context of religious significance
E is witnessed by multiple consistent observers
counterexample: Consider a case in which a large group of observers, gathered at a site of no religious significance, consistently reports that a heavy stone rose into the air and hung suspended for several minutes before gently descending. The event is incompatible with the known laws of nature, it is witnessed by multiple observers whose testimony is consistent, but it occurs at a secular gathering with no religious associations. The first and third conditions are met, but the second is not. Yet the event — a heavy stone defying gravity before a crowd of witnesses — might well be regarded as miraculous by those who experienced it, regardless of the secular context. The definition excludes events that possess the most striking feature of the miraculous — the violation of natural law — merely because they occur outside a religious setting.
abductive_insight: The background cases all occurred in contexts of religious significance, but the feature that made each seem miraculous was the violation of natural law, not the religious context. The definition has elevated a circumstantial feature of the paradigm cases — the religious setting — to the status of a necessary condition, when in fact the essential feature is the inexplicability of the event by natural means.
passage:
The events that religious traditions present as miracles — the parting of the Red Sea, the healing of the blind, the liquefaction of the blood of saints — share certain features. Each involves a physical occurrence that defies the known laws of nature: water divides, sight is restored to the congenitally blind, dried blood becomes liquid. Each occurs in a context of religious significance — at a moment of divine deliverance, in the presence of a holy figure, at a sacred festival. And each is attested by multiple witnesses. From these cases one might extract the definition: an event is a miracle if and only if it involves a physical occurrence incompatible with natural law, occurs in a religious context, and is witnessed by multiple consistent observers.

Yet this definition, drawn from cases that happen to be religious, proves too restrictive. Imagine a case in which a heavy stone rises into the air and hangs suspended for several minutes before gently descending to the ground, observed by a large crowd of people gathered for an entirely secular purpose — a market fair, let us say. The observers are numerous, sober, and consistent in their testimony. The event is wholly incompatible with the known laws of nature. The first and third conditions are met. But the event occurs in a secular context, and the second condition fails. Yet the event possesses precisely the feature that makes the paradigm cases seem miraculous: it is an occurrence that defies the natural order in a manner for which no natural explanation is available. The background cases all occurred in religious contexts, but it was not the religious context that made them miraculous — it was the violation of natural law. The definition has confused a feature of the circumstances in which the paradigm miracles happened to occur with a feature of the concept of miracle itself. What is needed is a definition that identifies the essential feature — the inexplicability of the event by natural means — without requiring that the event occur in a religious setting.

background_cases: Abraham's willingness to sacrifice Isaac, as narrated in Genesis, is taken as a paradigm of faith because Abraham trusted in the divine promise despite the apparent impossibility of its fulfilment, and acted upon that trust at great personal cost.
The early Christian martyrs are regarded as exemplars of faith because they maintained their commitment to their beliefs in the face of persecution and death, preferring suffering to apostasy.

Pascal's 'wager' presents a case in which faith is exercised as a practical commitment — a decision to live as though a divine being exists, undertaken in full awareness of the uncertainty of the belief, because the potential rewards of belief outweigh the potential costs of disbelief.
definition: A person S has faith if and only if (i) S holds a belief concerning a matter that transcends the reach of empirical verification, (ii) S maintains that belief with a degree of conviction that exceeds what the available evidence warrants, and (iii) S is prepared to act upon that belief.
conditions: S holds a belief concerning a matter beyond empirical verification
S maintains the belief with conviction exceeding the evidence
S is prepared to act upon the belief
counterexample: Consider a devoted follower of a charismatic political leader who holds, with conviction far exceeding the evidence, that the leader possesses extraordinary abilities, that the leader's programme will transform society, and that the leader cannot err. These beliefs transcend empirical verification in any practical sense, the follower's conviction exceeds the evidence, and the follower is prepared to act — to march, to sacrifice, to obey without question. All three conditions are met, yet we should hesitate to call this faith in the sense in which the term is used in religious and philosophical discourse. What the follower exhibits is fanaticism or credulity, not faith.
abductive_insight: The background cases — Abraham, the martyrs, Pascal — all involved a relationship of trust directed toward a transcendent reality, accompanied by a willingness to accept risk and uncertainty. The definition captures the excess of conviction over evidence and the readiness to act but drops the dimension of trust in a transcendent object — the orientation toward something beyond the human and the political that distinguishes faith from mere zealotry.
passage:
The paradigmatic cases of faith in the religious tradition — Abraham's trust in the divine promise, the steadfastness of the early martyrs, Pascal's wager — share a common structure. In each case, the person holds a belief about a matter that lies beyond empirical verification, maintains that belief with a conviction that outstrips the available evidence, and is prepared to act upon the belief at significant personal cost. Abraham believed in a promise that seemed impossible; the martyrs maintained their beliefs in the face of death; Pascal chose to live as though the divine existed despite the absence of proof. From these cases one might extract the definition: a person has faith if and only if the person holds a belief beyond empirical verification, with conviction exceeding the evidence, and is prepared to act upon it.

Yet this definition proves insufficient to distinguish faith from other states that share the same formal structure. Consider the devoted follower of a charismatic political leader — a follower who believes, with conviction far exceeding the evidence, that the leader possesses extraordinary capacities and that the leader's programme will transform society. The follower is prepared to act upon these beliefs — to march, to sacrifice, to obey without question. The beliefs transcend empirical verification; the conviction exceeds the evidence; the readiness to act is undeniable. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet what the follower exhibits is not faith but fanaticism, or perhaps credulity. The background cases differed in a crucial respect: in each, the object of trust was a transcendent reality — a divine being, a providential order, a ground of ultimate meaning. It was this orientation toward the transcendent that elevated conviction and commitment to the level of faith. The definition has abstracted the formal features of the paradigm cases — excess of conviction, readiness to act — but has dropped the essential feature: the direction of trust toward something that transcends the merely human and political.

background_cases: The Western Wall in Jerusalem is regarded as sacred because it is believed to be a remnant of the Temple, and those who approach it observe specific rituals — prayer, the placing of written petitions in the crevices — and feel a sense of awe that transforms the ordinary act of standing before a wall into an encounter with the transcendent.

The Ganges is regarded as sacred because it is associated with divine narratives and those who bathe in it believe themselves to be in contact with a purifying power that cannot be accounted for by the water's physical properties.

The bread and wine of the Eucharist are regarded as sacred because, in the context of the liturgy, they are held to be more than ordinary food and drink — they are believed to be, or to become, bearers of a divine presence.
definition: An object or place O is sacred if and only if (i) O is believed by a community to be a locus of a power or presence that transcends the ordinary, (ii) O is treated differently from ordinary objects — specific rituals, prohibitions, or forms of reverence are observed in relation to O, and (iii) O has been designated as sacred by an authorised religious institution or tradition.
conditions: O is believed to be a locus of transcendent power or presence
O is treated differently from ordinary objects through rituals and prohibitions
O has been designated sacred by an authorised religious institution
counterexample: Consider a natural spring in a remote region, to which local people have attributed healing powers for generations. They believe the spring is a locus of a power that transcends the ordinary — a power that cannot be explained by the water's mineral content. They treat the spring with reverence, observing specific rituals when they visit and prohibiting certain activities in its vicinity. But the spring has not been designated as sacred by any authorised religious institution; the practices surrounding it are folk traditions that have developed spontaneously and without institutional sanction. The first two conditions are met, but the third is not. Yet the spring functions as a sacred site for the community that reveres it — it is, in every phenomenological sense, a locus of the sacred.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the Western Wall, the Ganges, the Eucharist — are all associated with institutional traditions, but the feature that made each sacred was not the institutional designation per se but the experience of transcendent presence and the community's response to that experience. The definition has elevated the institutional dimension — which was present in the paradigm cases — to a necessary condition, when in fact sacredness may arise spontaneously from communal experience.
passage:
The objects and places that the great religious traditions hold sacred — the Western Wall, the Ganges, the bread and wine of the Eucharist — share certain features. Each is believed by a community to be the locus of a power or presence that transcends the ordinary. Each is treated with distinctive reverence — specific rituals, prohibitions, and forms of approach govern the community's dealings with it. And each has been recognised as sacred within an established religious tradition. From these cases one might extract the definition: an object or place is sacred if and only if it is believed to be a locus of transcendent power, is treated with special reverence, and has been designated sacred by an authorised religious institution.

Yet this definition, shaped by the paradigm cases of the great institutional religions, proves too restrictive. Consider a natural spring in a remote mountain valley, to which local people have attributed healing powers for centuries. They believe the spring to be the dwelling-place of a power that cannot be accounted for by the water's mineral content. They approach it with reverence, observing specific rituals — prayers, offerings, the removal of shoes. They observe prohibitions — no bathing is permitted, no washing of clothes, no grazing of animals in the vicinity. But the practices have developed spontaneously, without the sanction of any organised religion, and no institutional authority has designated the spring as sacred. The first two conditions are met, but the third is not. Yet the spring functions, for the community that reveres it, as a genuinely sacred site — a locus of the transcendent, set apart from the ordinary, commanding the awe and reverence that are the hallmarks of the sacred. The background cases happened to involve institutional recognition, but what made each sacred was not the institutional imprimatur — it was the community's experience of transcendent presence and its spontaneous response to that experience. The definition has confused a common accompaniment of sacredness with one of its necessary conditions.

background_cases: The cosmological argument, in its classical formulation, proceeds from the observation that contingent beings exist to the conclusion that a necessary being must exist as the ultimate ground of their existence; the argument derives its force from the principle that every contingent being requires a cause or explanation outside itself.

The teleological argument proceeds from the observation of order, purpose, and adaptation in nature to the conclusion that an intelligent designer is responsible; the argument derives its force from the analogy between natural contrivances and human artefacts.

The moral argument proceeds from the existence of objective moral obligations to the conclusion that a divine lawgiver must exist as the ground of those obligations; the argument derives its force from the claim that objective morality requires a transcendent source.
definition: An argument A for the existence of a divine being is rationally compelling if and only if (i) A proceeds from premises that are accepted by reasonable persons independently of the question of the divine being's existence, (ii) the conclusion follows from the premises by valid deductive or inductively strong reasoning, and (iii) no equally strong argument can be constructed for the negation of the conclusion.
conditions: A proceeds from independently accepted premises
The conclusion follows by valid or inductively strong reasoning
No equally strong argument exists for the negation of the conclusion
counterexample: Consider the following argument: every culture in recorded history has included belief in a divine being or beings; the universality of a belief among rational agents is evidence for its truth; therefore, a divine being probably exists. The first premise — the universality of theistic belief — is accepted by reasonable persons independently of the question of the divine being's existence; it is a sociological observation. The reasoning is inductively plausible: if a belief is universal, there may be something about the structure of reality that makes it natural and warranted. And it is not obvious that an equally strong argument can be constructed for the negation. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet the argument is weak: the universality of a belief may be explained by psychological, sociological, or evolutionary factors that have nothing to do with the belief's truth. The widespread belief in ghosts, in the flatness of the Earth, and in spontaneous generation were all universal at various times without being true.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the cosmological, teleological, and moral arguments — all derived their force not merely from the acceptability of their premises and the validity of their reasoning but from the difficulty of explaining the phenomena they cite (contingency, order, moral obligation) without recourse to the divine. The definition captures the formal requirements of a compelling argument but drops the requirement that the premises resist alternative explanation — that the phenomena cited cannot be adequately accounted for by non-theistic means.
passage:
The classical arguments for the existence of a divine being — the cosmological, the teleological, the moral — share a common structure. Each begins from premises that are accepted independently of the conclusion: the existence of contingent beings, the order of nature, the reality of moral obligation. Each proceeds by valid or inductively strong reasoning to the conclusion that a divine being exists. And each purports to be the best available explanation of the phenomena from which it proceeds. One might extract the definition: an argument for the divine being's existence is rationally compelling if and only if it proceeds from independently accepted premises, the conclusion follows by valid or strong reasoning, and no equally strong counterargument exists.

Yet this definition, though it captures the formal requirements of a compelling argument, proves insufficient. Consider the argument from the universality of theistic belief: every known culture has included belief in a divine being; the universality of a belief among rational agents is evidence for its truth; therefore, a divine being probably exists. The first premise is a sociological fact accepted independently of the conclusion. The reasoning is inductively plausible. And it is not immediately clear that an equally strong argument exists for the negation. All three conditions appear to be met. But the argument is unconvincing, because the universality of theistic belief can be explained without reference to its truth — by appeal to psychological dispositions, social functions, or evolutionary adaptations that would produce the belief whether or not a divine being existed. The background cases were stronger not merely in their formal structure but in the resistance of their premises to alternative explanation: it is genuinely difficult to explain why anything exists at all without invoking a necessary being; it is genuinely difficult to explain the fine adaptation of organisms without invoking some principle of order. The definition captures the formal properties of the arguments but drops the requirement that the phenomena cited resist non-theistic explanation — that the alternative accounts be less satisfactory than the theistic one.

background_cases: The free-will defence argues that the existence of moral evil — evil caused by the deliberate choices of human agents — is compatible with divine goodness because free will is a good so great that it is worth the risk of its misuse; the defence succeeds insofar as the good of freedom genuinely requires the possibility of wrongdoing.

The soul-making theodicy argues that suffering provides the conditions necessary for the development of virtues such as courage, compassion, and fortitude; the argument succeeds insofar as these virtues could not be developed in a world without genuine hardship.

The greater-good defence argues that certain specific instances of suffering are justified because they are necessary conditions for goods that outweigh them — as, for example, the pain of surgery is justified by the health it restores.
definition: A theodicy T is adequate if and only if (i) T identifies a specific good or set of goods that requires the existence of suffering as a necessary condition, (ii) T demonstrates that the goods in question are of sufficient value to outweigh the suffering, and (iii) T is consistent with the attributes traditionally ascribed to the divine being — omnipotence, omniscience, and perfect goodness.
conditions: T identifies goods requiring suffering as a necessary condition
T demonstrates the goods outweigh the suffering
T is consistent with the traditional divine attributes
counterexample: Consider a theodicy that invokes aesthetic value: the universe, like a great work of art, requires contrast and discord to achieve its overall beauty, and suffering provides the dark tones against which the bright tones of joy and goodness are displayed. Aesthetic value is a genuine good; the theodicy claims that the beauty of the whole outweighs the suffering of the parts; and the theodicy is consistent with traditional divine attributes, since an omnipotent, omniscient, and perfectly good being might create the most beautiful universe possible. All three conditions are met. Yet the theodicy seems deeply inadequate, for it treats the suffering of sentient beings as a means to an aesthetic end — as the dark pigment in a cosmic painting — and thereby fails to take seriously the suffering of the individual who endures it.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the free-will defence, the soul-making theodicy, the greater-good defence — all involved goods that accrued, at least in part, to the sufferers themselves: the freedom that makes wrongdoing possible is a good for the free agent; the virtues that suffering develops are goods for the person who develops them; the health that surgery restores benefits the patient. The definition requires that the goods outweigh the suffering but does not require that the goods bear any relation to the sufferers. An aesthetic theodicy treats sufferers as instruments of cosmic beauty — means to an end that is not their own — and thereby violates the moral intuition that is at the heart of the original difficulty.
passage:
The classical theodicies — the free-will defence, the soul-making theodicy, the greater-good defence — share a common strategy. Each identifies a good that requires the possibility or actuality of suffering, argues that the good outweighs the suffering, and demonstrates consistency with the traditional divine attributes. The free-will defence identifies the good of freedom, which requires the possibility of its misuse. The soul-making theodicy identifies the good of moral development, which requires genuine hardship. The greater-good defence identifies specific goods that outweigh specific evils. From these cases one might extract the definition: a theodicy is adequate if and only if it identifies goods requiring suffering, demonstrates that the goods outweigh the suffering, and is consistent with divine omnipotence, omniscience, and perfect goodness.

Yet this definition, though it captures the structure of the classical theodicies, admits responses that are deeply troubling. Consider an aesthetic theodicy: the universe, like a great painting, requires contrast and discord to achieve its full beauty, and suffering supplies the shadows without which the light of joy and goodness would be invisible. Aesthetic value is a genuine good. The beauty of the cosmos as a whole might, on some accounting, outweigh the suffering of its parts. And the creation of the most beautiful universe is consistent with the attributes of an omnipotent, omniscient, and perfectly good being. All three conditions are met. But the theodicy is morally repugnant, for it treats the agony of sentient beings as raw material for cosmic beautification — as the dark pigment in a divine canvas. The background cases were more satisfactory because the goods they identified accrued, at least in part, to the sufferers themselves: freedom benefits the free agent; virtue benefits the person who develops it; restored health benefits the patient. The aesthetic theodicy directs the good away from the sufferer entirely. The definition requires that goods outweigh suffering but does not require that the goods bear any relation to those who suffer — a silence that permits theodicies in which persons are treated as means rather than as ends.

background_cases: The prohibition against murder, which is present in virtually every moral and religious code, is taken by the divine command tradition as an instance in which the divine will and moral intuition coincide: the action prohibited is one that all persons, on reflection, would recognise as wrong, and the divine command confirms and reinforces what reason independently suggests.

The obligation to honour one's parents, similarly present across many traditions, is taken as an instance in which the divine command articulates a duty that human beings can recognise as binding on grounds of gratitude, care, and social order, independently of the command.

The obligation of truthfulness — the prohibition against bearing false witness — is taken as a case in which the divine command gives authoritative expression to a principle that human reason can independently grasp, since the social life of rational beings depends upon mutual trust.
definition: An action A is morally right if and only if (i) A is commanded or approved by a divine being, (ii) the divine being's command is motivated by the goodness of the action — that is, the divine being commands A because A is good, and (iii) human beings are capable of recognising the goodness of A independently of the divine command.
conditions: A is commanded or approved by a divine being
The divine being commands A because A is good
Human beings can recognise the goodness of A independently of the command
counterexample: Consider the dietary laws observed in certain religious traditions — prohibitions against eating certain animals, requirements to prepare food in prescribed ways, rules governing the combination of foods at a single meal. These prescriptions are commanded by the divine being and are taken by the faithful as morally binding. But it is far from clear that the divine being commands these practices because they are independently good; their moral force appears to derive entirely from the divine command rather than from any goodness recognisable by unaided reason. A non-adherent, examining the dietary laws without reference to the divine command, would find no moral reason to comply. The first condition is met, but the second and third are doubtful. Yet these dietary prescriptions are regarded by their adherents as among the most important moral obligations, and the divine command theory was intended, in part, to account for precisely such obligations.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the prohibitions against murder and false witness, the obligation to honour one's parents — were all cases in which the divine command and independent moral reasoning converge: the commanded actions are recognisable as good on grounds that have nothing to do with their being commanded. The definition has been shaped by these convergent cases and fails to account for obligations — such as dietary and ritual prescriptions — whose bindingness appears to derive entirely from the divine command, without any independent moral ground. The theory was partly designed to explain such obligations, yet the definition, by requiring independent recognisability, excludes them.
passage:
The divine command tradition in moral philosophy has always drawn much of its plausibility from cases in which the divine will and independent moral judgement coincide. The prohibition against murder is commanded by the divine being, but it is also recognisable as right by unaided reason. The obligation to honour one's parents is divinely ordained, but it is also supported by considerations of gratitude and social order. The prohibition against lying is a divine command, but it is also demanded by the conditions of social trust. In these cases, the divine command confirms what reason independently grasps. One might be tempted to define moral rightness as the conjunction of divine command, divine motivation by the goodness of the action, and the independent recognisability of that goodness.

Yet this definition, shaped by cases of convergence, fails to accommodate a large and important class of moral obligations that the divine command theory was designed to explain. Consider the dietary laws observed in certain traditions — the prohibition against eating specified animals, the requirements for the preparation and combination of foods. These prescriptions are unambiguously commanded by the divine being. They are regarded by the faithful as morally binding in the strongest sense. But their goodness is not recognisable independently of the command. A person who examined the dietary laws without any acquaintance with or commitment to the divine command would find no moral reason to abstain from the prohibited foods; the prohibitions would appear arbitrary. The second and third conditions are doubtful: it is not clear that the divine being commands these practices because they are independently good, and human beings cannot recognise their goodness without reference to the command. The background cases were all cases of convergence — cases in which divine command and independent reason pointed in the same direction. The definition has generalised from these convergent cases and thereby excluded the very obligations — ritual, dietary, ceremonial — whose bindingness the divine command theory was partly intended to explain: obligations whose moral force derives not from their independent goodness but solely from the authority of the one who commands them.

background_cases: The cosmological argument in its classical form — that everything that begins to exist has a cause, the universe began to exist, therefore the universe has a cause — derives its force from the principle that the conclusion identifies an entity whose existence is required by the premises, not merely permitted by them.

The design argument in its Paleyan form — that the complexity of organisms implies a designer — derives its force from the claim that the conclusion (a designer exists) is required by the best explanation of the observed complexity.

The moral argument — that the existence of objective moral obligations implies a moral lawgiver — derives its force from the claim that the conclusion is the only adequate explanation of the moral phenomena.
definition: An argument A provides evidence for the existence of God if and only if (i) A starts from premises about the natural world that are widely accepted, (ii) A shows that these premises are best explained by the hypothesis that God exists, and (iii) the hypothesis is not ad hoc — that is, it has independent motivation beyond explaining the premises in question.
conditions: A starts from widely accepted premises about the natural world
A shows that these premises are best explained by the God hypothesis
The hypothesis is not ad hoc
counterexample: Consider the argument from religious experience: many people across many cultures report experiences of the divine — a sense of presence, of awe, of communion with a transcendent being. This is a widely accepted fact about the natural world. One might argue that the best explanation of these experiences is that God exists and causes them. And the God hypothesis has independent motivation from other arguments. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet the argument is undermined by the availability of naturalistic explanations — psychological, neurological, sociological — that account for the experiences without positing a divine cause. The definition's second condition requires that the God hypothesis be the best explanation, but it provides no criterion for adjudicating between rival explanations of equal or near-equal plausibility.
abductive_insight: The background cases — cosmological, design, moral — all operate in domains where the available alternative explanations are limited or clearly inferior. The definition captures the inferential structure but fails to require that alternative explanations be shown to be inadequate, which matters precisely in cases where plausible naturalistic alternatives are available.
passage:
The arguments for the existence of God that are most widely regarded as forceful share a common inferential structure. The cosmological argument moves from the existence of contingent beings to a necessary being as the required explanation. The design argument moves from the complexity of natural organisms to an intelligent designer as the best explanation. The moral argument moves from the reality of objective moral obligations to a moral lawgiver as the only adequate ground. In each case, the argument purports to show that the divine hypothesis is not merely compatible with the data but required by them, and that no rival hypothesis is equally adequate.

One might codify this structure by defining evidential support for theism as follows: an argument provides evidence for God's existence if it starts from widely accepted premises about the natural world, shows that these premises are best explained by the God hypothesis, and the hypothesis is independently motivated. This definition captures the inferential shape of the paradigm cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is the argument from religious experience. The premise is widely accepted: a great many people across diverse cultures and epochs report experiences they describe as encounters with the divine. One might argue that the best explanation is that God exists and is the cause of these experiences, and that the God hypothesis has independent motivation from the cosmological and moral arguments. All three conditions appear satisfied. But the argument is vulnerable in a way that the paradigm cases are not. Naturalistic explanations of religious experience — in terms of neural activity, psychological need, social conditioning, or altered states of consciousness — are available and well-developed. The definition requires that the God hypothesis be the best explanation, but it provides no criterion for determining when a hypothesis is best in the presence of plausible competitors. The background cases succeeded because the rival explanations were clearly inferior; the religious-experience case reveals that without a criterion for adjudicating among competitors, the definition's second condition is indeterminate.

background_cases: The parting of the Red Sea, as described in Exodus, is regarded as a paradigmatic miracle because no natural process known to the ancient Israelites could have produced such an event; its miraculous character derives from its falling outside the range of natural possibility.

The resurrection narratives in the Gospels are regarded as miraculous because the restoration of a dead person to life is contrary to every known biological process; the event is significant precisely because it could not have occurred naturally.
The tradition of Muhammad's night journey is regarded as miraculous because the physical transportation of a human being from Mecca to Jerusalem in a single night lies beyond natural possibility.
definition: An event E is a miracle if and only if (i) E is contrary to the known laws of nature, (ii) E is attributed to divine agency, and (iii) E is attested by credible testimony.
conditions: E is contrary to the known laws of nature
E is attributed to divine agency
E is attested by credible testimony
counterexample: Consider the Eucharistic transformation in Catholic doctrine: the bread and wine are said to become the body and blood of Christ while retaining every physical property of bread and wine. The event is attributed to divine agency. It is attested by the continuous testimony of the Church, which many would regard as credible. Yet the event is not contrary to any known law of nature, for no observable physical change occurs — every chemical analysis, every sensory examination, would confirm that the substances remain bread and wine. The first condition is not satisfied, yet Catholic theology regards the Eucharistic transformation as one of the most important miracles.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the Red Sea, the Resurrection, the Night Journey — all involve events that manifestly contravene natural processes in an observable way. The definition captures this observable contravention as necessary, but the Eucharistic case shows that a tradition may regard an event as miraculous precisely because the transformation is metaphysical rather than physical, eluding empirical detection while remaining theologically central.
passage:
The miracles that figure most prominently in the major religious traditions share a distinctive feature. The parting of the Red Sea manifestly contravened the natural behaviour of bodies of water. The resurrection of Christ contravened the known biological process by which death is irreversible. Muhammad's night journey contravened the physical constraints on human transportation. In each case, the miracle's claim to be miraculous rested upon its visible departure from the natural order — an event occurred that could not have occurred by natural means, and its impossibility under natural law was plain to observers.

One might distil from these cases the following definition: an event is a miracle if and only if it is contrary to the known laws of nature, attributed to divine agency, and attested by credible testimony. This definition captures the structure of the paradigm cases faithfully. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is furnished by the Catholic doctrine of the Eucharistic transformation. According to this doctrine, the bread and wine of the Eucharist become, in their substance, the body and blood of Christ, while all their sensible accidents — their appearance, taste, texture, and chemical composition — remain unchanged. The transformation is attributed to divine agency operating through the words of the priest. It is attested by the continuous and authoritative testimony of the Catholic Church. The second and third conditions are met. But the first condition is not: no law of nature is contravened, for no observable change takes place. Every physical test would confirm that the substance on the altar has the properties of bread and wine. Yet the Church regards this transformation as among the most solemn and important of miracles. The definition, built from cases where the miraculous was empirically manifest, cannot accommodate a miracle that is metaphysical in character and empirically undetectable.

background_cases: Abraham's faith, as described in Genesis, is paradigmatic because he trusted in God's promise despite the absence of supporting evidence and in the face of apparent impossibility — he was told he would be the father of nations though aged and childless.

The faith of the early Christian martyrs is regarded as exemplary because they maintained their conviction in the face of persecution, torture, and death, when all worldly evidence counselled recantation.
Kierkegaard's knight of faith makes a 'leap' precisely because the rational evidence is insufficient — faith, in Kierkegaard's account, begins where reason and evidence give out.
definition: A person S has faith that p if and only if (i) S believes that p, (ii) S lacks sufficient evidence to establish p, and (iii) S's belief that p is held with a degree of commitment proportional to the importance S attaches to p.
conditions: S believes that p
S lacks sufficient evidence to establish p
S's commitment to p is proportional to the importance S attaches to p
counterexample: Consider a scientist who believes that her theory is correct despite the fact that the experimental evidence is, at present, inconclusive. She lacks sufficient evidence to establish the theory. She is deeply committed to it — she has staked her career upon it and pursues it with single-minded devotion. Her commitment is proportional to the enormous importance she attaches to the theory. All three conditions are met. Yet one would hesitate to say that she has faith in the theory in the sense that Abraham had faith in God's promise. She expects that further evidence will vindicate her; her attitude is one of confident anticipation rather than trust in the face of darkness.
abductive_insight: The background cases — Abraham, the martyrs, Kierkegaard's knight of faith — all involve a willingness to maintain belief not merely in the absence of evidence but in the face of evidence or circumstances that weigh against the belief. The definition captures the evidential deficit and the commitment but fails to require that the believer persist against countervailing evidence or apparent impossibility, which is the distinctive mark of faith in the paradigm cases.
passage:
The paradigmatic cases of faith in the Western tradition share a feature that distinguishes them from mere hopeful belief. Abraham trusted in God's promise that he would father nations despite being aged and childless — the evidence pointed against the promise, yet he believed. The early Christian martyrs maintained their convictions under torture and the threat of death — every worldly consideration counselled recantation, yet they persisted. Kierkegaard's knight of faith makes his leap precisely at the point where rational evidence runs out and the absurd begins. In each case, faith involves not merely believing without sufficient evidence but believing against the weight of countervailing evidence or circumstance.

One might attempt to capture these cases by defining faith as belief held without sufficient evidence and with commitment proportional to the importance attached to the proposition. This definition accommodates the evidential gap and the intensity of commitment evident in the paradigm cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is provided by the committed scientist. Consider a researcher who believes that her theory of stellar formation is correct, despite the fact that the experimental evidence is at present inconclusive. She lacks sufficient evidence to establish the theory. She is deeply committed — she has devoted years of her career to it and pursues it with a devotion proportional to its importance in her intellectual life. All three conditions are formally met. Yet there is a crucial difference between her attitude and Abraham's. The scientist expects that further evidence will vindicate her theory; her commitment rests on the confidence that the evidential gap will close. Abraham had no such expectation — the impossibility was not a temporary deficit but a permanent feature of the situation, and his faith consisted in trusting despite that impossibility. The definition captures the absence of evidence but not the presence of countervailing evidence, which is what gives the paradigmatic cases of faith their distinctive character.

background_cases: The cosmological argument moves from the existence of contingent beings to the existence of a necessary being, and its force depends upon the assumption that an infinite regress of contingent causes is impossible — that the chain of causation must terminate in something that exists necessarily.

The teleological argument moves from the appearance of design in nature to the existence of a designer, and its force depends upon the assumption that the order and complexity of nature are best explained by intentional arrangement rather than by unguided natural processes.

The ontological argument moves from the concept of a maximally great being to the existence of such a being, and its force depends upon the assumption that existence is a perfection — that a being possessing all perfections must exist, since otherwise it would lack a perfection.
definition: An argument for the existence of God is sound if and only if (i) its premises are true, (ii) its reasoning is deductively valid, and (iii) no equally plausible alternative conclusion can be drawn from the same premises.
conditions: The premises are true
The reasoning is deductively valid
No equally plausible alternative conclusion follows from the same premises
counterexample: Consider the argument from the fine-tuning of physical constants: the fundamental constants of the universe — the gravitational constant, the strong nuclear force, the cosmological constant — are set within an extraordinarily narrow range that permits the existence of complex matter and life. The premises appear true: the fine-tuning is widely acknowledged by physicists. One might construct a valid deductive argument from these premises to the conclusion that the constants were set by a divine agent. But an equally plausible alternative conclusion — that our universe is one member of a vast multiverse, and we necessarily observe a universe whose constants permit our existence — can be drawn from the same premises. Condition (iii) is not met, yet the fine-tuning argument remains one of the most actively debated arguments in the philosophy of religion.
abductive_insight: The background cases — cosmological, teleological, ontological — all succeed or fail to the extent that their premises compel a unique conclusion. The definition captures this requirement in condition (iii), but the fine-tuning case shows that when the premises are consistent with multiple explanatory frameworks, the question of which conclusion is most plausible cannot be settled by the logical structure of the argument alone — it requires substantive metaphysical commitments that the argument itself does not adjudicate.
passage:
The arguments for the existence of God that have commanded the most sustained philosophical attention share a common aspiration: to move from premises that are widely accepted to a conclusion that is uniquely supported by those premises. The cosmological argument aspires to show that the existence of contingent beings requires a necessary being, with no alternative terminus for the chain of explanation. The teleological argument aspires to show that the order of nature requires a designer, with no alternative source of the observed complexity. The ontological argument aspires to show that the concept of maximal greatness entails existence, with no alternative interpretation of the concept.

One might codify this aspiration by defining a sound argument for God's existence as one whose premises are true, whose reasoning is valid, and from whose premises no equally plausible alternative conclusion can be drawn. This definition captures the logical requirements and adds a condition of uniqueness that is meant to ensure the argument compels its conclusion. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition is the argument from the fine-tuning of physical constants. The fundamental constants of physics — the gravitational constant, the strength of the electromagnetic force, the cosmological constant — are set within an astonishingly narrow range compatible with the existence of complex matter and, ultimately, life. Small variations would have produced a universe of nothing but hydrogen, or a universe that collapsed within moments. These premises are widely accepted by physicists. One might construct a valid argument from them to the conclusion that the constants were deliberately set by a purposive agent. But the same premises are equally consistent with the multiverse hypothesis — the proposal that our universe is one of an enormous number of universes, each with different constants, and that we necessarily find ourselves in one where the constants permit our existence. The theistic and the multiverse conclusions are drawn from the same premises with comparable plausibility, and the choice between them depends not on the argument's structure but on prior metaphysical commitments that the argument cannot adjudicate.

background_cases: The free-will defence argues that the existence of moral evil — the cruelty and wickedness of human beings — is compatible with a perfectly good God because free will is a great good that necessarily carries with it the possibility of its misuse; God could not have created free beings who were guaranteed to choose rightly.

The soul-making theodicy argues that suffering is compatible with God's goodness because the development of moral character — courage, compassion, patience — requires exposure to hardship and adversity; a world without suffering would be a world without the possibility of moral growth.

The greater-good defence argues that specific evils may be permitted by God because they are necessary conditions for goods that outweigh them — the suffering of the martyr, for instance, makes possible the virtue of heroic faith.
definition: A theodicy T successfully reconciles the existence of evil with the existence of a perfectly good, omnipotent God if and only if (i) T identifies a good G that is made possible by the existence of evil, (ii) G is of sufficient value to outweigh the evil in question, and (iii) T shows that God could not have achieved G without permitting the evil.
conditions: T identifies a good G made possible by the existence of evil
G is of sufficient value to outweigh the evil
T shows God could not have achieved G without permitting the evil
counterexample: Consider the suffering of an infant who dies of a painful disease within weeks of birth. The free-will defence does not apply, for the suffering is not the result of any free agent's choice. The soul-making theodicy does not apply, for an infant cannot undergo moral development. The greater-good defence requires that some good G be identified that is made possible by the infant's suffering, that G outweigh the suffering, and that God could not have achieved G without it. But it is extraordinarily difficult to identify any good that requires the suffering of an infant who will never grow to be an agent, never develop character, never know that a good has come from the suffering. All three conditions of the definition may be formally met if one posits that the suffering contributes to a cosmic good beyond human comprehension, but this move renders the theodicy unfalsifiable and indistinguishable from the absence of any explanation at all.
abductive_insight: The background cases — free will, soul-making, greater good — all succeed in identifying goods that bear a comprehensible relationship to the evils they are invoked to justify: the misuse of freedom explains moral evil, hardship explains the possibility of character, suffering explains heroic virtue. The definition captures the structure of these justifications but cannot distinguish between a theodicy that identifies a comprehensible connection between evil and good and one that merely posits an incomprehensible connection. When the evil is of a kind that resists connection to any identifiable good — as in the suffering of an infant — the definition permits the theodicist to gesture at a good beyond human comprehension, but this gesture evacuates the theodicy of its explanatory content.
passage:
The theodicies that are most widely regarded as at least partially successful share a common feature: the good they identify bears a comprehensible relationship to the evil it is invoked to justify. The free-will defence identifies a connection between moral evil and the great good of free agency — the evil is the predictable consequence of the good's exercise. The soul-making theodicy identifies a connection between suffering and moral development — the evil is the necessary condition of the good's realisation. The greater-good defence identifies a connection between particular sufferings and particular goods that outweigh them. In each case, one can see why the evil and the good are linked.

One might codify these cases by defining a successful theodicy as one that identifies a good made possible by the evil, of sufficient value to outweigh it, that God could not have achieved without permitting the evil. This definition captures the structure of the paradigm cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is the suffering of infants. Consider an infant born with a painful degenerative condition who dies after weeks of suffering, having never achieved consciousness sufficient for moral development, free choice, or the comprehension of any good that might come from the suffering. The free-will defence is inapplicable: no free agent caused the condition. The soul-making theodicy is inapplicable: no soul was formed. The greater-good defence requires that some good be identified that is made possible by this particular suffering and could not have been achieved without it. The theodicist may posit that the suffering contributes to a cosmic good beyond human comprehension — perhaps the completion of a divine plan whose purposes we cannot fathom. The definition's three conditions are formally met if one grants this posit. But the posit is empty: it provides no comprehensible connection between the evil and the good, and it is indistinguishable in practice from the admission that no explanation is available. The background cases all involved connections between evil and good that could be understood. The definition permits theodicies that merely postulate such connections without rendering them intelligible.

background_cases: The prohibition against murder is regarded as a clear case of a divine command that is also independently morally binding: God commands that one shall not murder, and the wrongness of murder is recognisable by any reflective moral agent independently of the command.

The command to honour one's parents is regarded as a case where divine authority reinforces an obligation that is also recognisable by natural moral reflection — the debt of gratitude and care owed by children to those who raised them.

The command to refrain from bearing false witness is regarded as a case where the divine prohibition aligns with the independently recognisable moral requirement of honesty in matters affecting the welfare and reputation of others.
definition: A moral obligation obtains if and only if (i) God commands the action in question, (ii) the agent has access to the command through scripture or authoritative tradition, and (iii) the agent is able to comply with the command.
conditions: God commands the action
The agent has access to the command through scripture or tradition
The agent is able to comply
counterexample: Consider the case of a person living in classical Athens in the fifth century B.C., centuries before the composition of the Christian scriptures and outside the reach of Judaic tradition. This person recognises that it is wrong to torture a slave for amusement — a conviction shared by some, though not all, of her contemporaries. Her moral conviction is not derived from any divine command communicated through scripture or authoritative tradition; the available religious traditions of her culture impose no such prohibition. Condition (ii) is not satisfied. Yet it seems clear that the obligation not to torture for amusement obtains regardless of whether a divine command has been communicated, and that the Athenian who recognises this is responding to a genuine moral requirement.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the prohibition of murder, the duty to honour parents, the prohibition of false witness — all involve commands that align with obligations independently recognisable by moral reflection. The definition treats the divine command and its communication as the source of the obligation, but the background cases succeed precisely because the obligations are also recognisable without the command. The Athenian case reveals that the definition cannot account for moral obligations that obtain in the absence of any communicated divine command, suggesting that the ground of moral obligation cannot be located solely in divine communication.
passage:
The moral commands that figure most prominently in the major theistic traditions share a notable feature: the obligations they impose are also recognisable by independent moral reflection. The prohibition of murder is a divine command, but the wrongness of murder is apparent to any reflective moral agent quite apart from scriptural authority. The duty to honour one's parents is divinely commanded, but the obligation of gratitude and care is recognisable without revelation. The prohibition of false testimony is divinely imposed, but the requirements of honesty in matters affecting others are independently discernible. In each case, the divine command reinforces an obligation that reason can discover on its own.

One might codify the relationship between divine command and moral obligation as follows: a moral obligation obtains if and only if God commands the action, the agent has access to the command through scripture or tradition, and the agent is able to comply. This definition makes the divine command and its communication the joint source of moral obligation. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is the moral life of the classical Athenian. Consider a woman in fifth-century Athens who recognises — as some of her contemporaries did — that the torture of slaves for amusement is morally abhorrent. She has no access to the Hebrew scriptures, no acquaintance with the Christian or Islamic traditions, no authoritative religious tradition that communicates a divine prohibition of this practice. Condition (ii) is not met. The definition implies that she is under no moral obligation to refrain from torture, since no divine command has been communicated to her. But this conclusion is intolerable. The obligation not to inflict gratuitous suffering is, one feels, not contingent upon the receipt of a divine communication. The background cases all involved obligations that were recognisable independently of the command. The definition, by locating the ground of obligation exclusively in the communicated command, cannot account for the moral convictions of those who stand outside the reach of any scriptural or traditional authority.

background_cases: The cosmological argument reasons from the existence of contingent beings to the necessity of a first cause, and its persuasive force depends upon the claim that an infinite regress of contingent causes is impossible — a claim that can be assessed independently of one's prior religious commitments; the argument submits itself to rational scrutiny on grounds that both theist and atheist can share.

The teleological argument reasons from the apparent design in nature to the existence of a designer, and its persuasive force depends upon the claim that complex order requires an intelligent source — a claim that can be assessed by examining the natural world and considering alternative explanations; the argument appeals to considerations accessible to all rational agents.

The moral argument reasons from the existence of objective moral obligations to the existence of a moral lawgiver, and its persuasive force depends upon the claim that moral obligations require a transcendent ground — a claim that can be debated on philosophical grounds without reference to religious authority or personal experience.
definition: An argument A for the existence of God is rationally compelling if and only if (i) the premises of A are acceptable to any rational agent regardless of prior religious commitment, (ii) the inference from premises to conclusion is logically valid, and (iii) the conclusion asserts the existence of a being recognisable as God.
conditions: The premises are acceptable to any rational agent regardless of prior religious commitment
The inference from premises to conclusion is logically valid
The conclusion asserts the existence of a being recognisable as God
counterexample: Consider the argument from religious experience: millions of persons across diverse cultures and centuries have reported experiences of a divine presence — experiences that are phenomenologically vivid, transformative of character, and remarkably convergent in their core features. The premise that such experiences occur is acceptable to any rational agent; the inference to a divine cause is logically valid given the auxiliary premise that the best explanation of widespread, convergent experience is the reality of its object; and the conclusion asserts the existence of God. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet the argument is not rationally compelling in the way the definition intends, for the auxiliary premise — that the best explanation of convergent religious experience is the reality of its object — is precisely the sort of claim that a rational agent may reasonably reject without irrationality.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve arguments whose premises and inferential principles can be assessed on neutral philosophical grounds — the impossibility of infinite regress, the need for an intelligent source of complexity, the requirement of a transcendent ground for morality. The definition captures this neutrality as a requirement. Yet the argument from religious experience reveals that an argument may employ premises acceptable to all rational agents whilst relying on an inferential principle — the explanatory significance of convergent experience — that reasonable persons may dispute. The definition fails to distinguish between the acceptability of premises and the compellingness of the inferential step.
passage:
The most respected arguments for the existence of God share a distinctive feature: they appeal to considerations that any rational agent can assess without relying upon prior religious commitment. The cosmological argument rests upon the apparent impossibility of an infinite regress of contingent causes. The teleological argument rests upon the observation of complex order in nature. The moral argument rests upon the apparent objectivity of moral obligations. In each case, the premises can be evaluated on philosophical grounds that theist and atheist share.

One might codify this feature in the following definition: an argument for the existence of God is rationally compelling if and only if its premises are acceptable to any rational agent regardless of prior religious commitment, the inference is logically valid, and the conclusion asserts the existence of a being recognisable as God. This definition captures the rational accessibility of the paradigmatic arguments. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is the argument from religious experience. The premise of this argument — that millions of persons across diverse cultures have reported vivid, transformative experiences of a divine presence — is a matter of empirical fact acceptable to any rational agent. The inference — that the best explanation of such widespread, convergent experience is the reality of its object — is logically valid given the auxiliary premise about explanation. The conclusion asserts the existence of God. All three conditions appear to be satisfied. Yet the argument is not rationally compelling in the manner the definition intends. A rational agent may acknowledge the experiential data whilst reasonably maintaining that the convergence is better explained by features of human psychology than by the existence of a divine object. One might observe that what the background cases possess, and what the argument from religious experience lacks, is an inferential principle whose denial would be unreasonable. The definition captures the accessibility of premises but not the compellingness of the inferential step.

background_cases: A scientist who accepts the atomic theory of matter does so on the basis of evidence — the gas laws, Brownian motion, chemical stoichiometry — and her acceptance is proportioned to the evidence; she would revise her view if the evidence changed, and her acceptance is characteristically tentative and revisable.

A mathematician who accepts the truth of a theorem does so on the basis of proof, and her acceptance is determined by the validity of the proof; she would withdraw her acceptance if an error in the proof were discovered, and her acceptance is characteristically conditional upon the integrity of the demonstration.

A historian who accepts that Caesar crossed the Rubicon does so on the basis of documentary evidence — the testimony of ancient sources, the coherence of the narrative, the absence of contradictory evidence — and her acceptance is proportioned to the strength of the evidence; she would revise her view if new evidence emerged.
definition: A person S has faith in a proposition p if and only if (i) S believes that p, (ii) S's belief is not proportioned to the available evidence, and (iii) S maintains the belief despite the recognition that the evidence is insufficient.
conditions: S believes that p
S's belief is not proportioned to the available evidence
S maintains the belief despite recognising that evidence is insufficient
counterexample: Consider a parent whose child has gone missing and who maintains faith that the child is alive despite the mounting evidence — the passage of time, the failure of search parties, the statistical improbability — that the child has perished. The parent believes the child is alive, the belief is not proportioned to the evidence, and the parent maintains it in full recognition that the evidence is against her. All three conditions are met. Yet one might hesitate to assimilate this case to religious faith. The parent's faith is sustained not by any theological conviction or spiritual discipline but by the sheer force of parental love and the psychological impossibility of accepting the alternative. Her faith is not a cognitive stance toward a proposition but an expression of a relationship.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve acceptance that is characteristically propositional and evidence-responsive — the scientist, the mathematician, and the historian accept propositions on the basis of evidence or proof and would revise their acceptance accordingly. The definition of faith is constructed as the negation of this pattern: belief that is not proportioned to evidence.
