Yet the parent's case reveals that not all belief that exceeds the evidence is of the same kind. What distinguishes religious faith from the parent's desperate hope is not the relation to evidence but the nature of the commitment — religious faith characteristically involves trust in a person or a tradition, not merely disproportionate belief in a proposition.
passage:
The forms of acceptance most characteristic of rational inquiry share a common structure. The scientist accepts the atomic theory because the evidence supports it and would revise her view if the evidence changed. The mathematician accepts a theorem because a valid proof has been given and would withdraw acceptance if an error were found. The historian accepts that Caesar crossed the Rubicon because the documentary evidence supports the claim. In each case, acceptance is proportioned to evidence and is characteristically revisable.

One might define faith as the negation of this pattern: a person has faith in a proposition if and only if she believes it, her belief is not proportioned to the available evidence, and she maintains the belief despite recognising the insufficiency of the evidence. This definition captures the common intuition that faith involves believing beyond or against the evidence. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is that of a parent whose child has gone missing. As weeks pass without word, the evidence increasingly points toward the worst conclusion. Yet the parent maintains faith that the child is alive, in full awareness that the evidence is against her. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet one should hesitate to identify this faith with religious faith. The parent does not believe on the basis of theological conviction, spiritual practice, or trust in a religious tradition. Her belief is sustained by the force of parental love and the psychological impossibility of accepting the loss. One might observe that the definition, by characterising faith solely in terms of its relation to evidence, fails to capture the distinctive character of religious faith, which involves not merely disproportionate belief but a mode of trust — trust in a person, a tradition, or a way of life — that is not reducible to a propositional attitude.

background_cases: The suffering caused by human cruelty — warfare, torture, oppression — is sometimes defended by the free-will theodicy, which holds that a world containing free agents who can choose to do evil is more valuable than a world of automata incapable of moral choice; the suffering is a consequence of the misuse of a genuine good, namely free will.

The suffering caused by natural disasters — earthquakes, floods, volcanic eruptions — is sometimes defended by the soul-making theodicy, which holds that a world containing natural hazards provides the conditions necessary for the development of courage, compassion, and resilience; the suffering serves the purpose of moral and spiritual growth.

The suffering of the innocent in cases of disease is sometimes defended by the greater-good theodicy, which holds that individual instances of suffering may be necessary components of a larger providential plan whose goodness is not fully visible from the finite human perspective; the suffering is justified by goods that we cannot presently discern.
definition: The existence of suffering S is compatible with the existence of an omnipotent, omnibenevolent God if and only if (i) S serves a morally sufficient reason or greater good, (ii) an omnipotent God could not have achieved the greater good without permitting S, and (iii) the greater good outweighs S.
conditions: S serves a morally sufficient reason or greater good
An omnipotent God could not have achieved the greater good without permitting S
The greater good outweighs S
counterexample: Consider the suffering of a very young infant who dies of a painful congenital disease within days of birth, before any capacity for moral development, learning, or spiritual growth has emerged. The infant's suffering does not serve the free-will defence, for the infant has made no free choice. It does not serve the soul-making defence, for the infant has no opportunity for moral or spiritual growth. The greater-good defence may be invoked, but to assert that this suffering is a necessary component of a plan whose goodness outweighs it, when the sufferer derives no benefit whatever, is to strain the concept of moral sufficiency beyond what we should be willing to accept.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve suffering that can be connected, however tenuously, to a recognisable good — the value of free will, the development of character, the possibility of a larger providential plan. The definition codifies this pattern by requiring that suffering serve a morally sufficient reason. Yet the case of the dying infant reveals suffering that resists connection to any recognisable good available to the sufferer. The theodicies work by identifying goods that accrue to the agents involved — the exercise of free will, the growth of character — but the infant is not an agent and accrues no good. The definition fails to require that the greater good be in some way related to the sufferer, and without this requirement, any suffering whatever can be declared compatible with divine goodness by appeal to goods entirely external to the one who suffers.
passage:
The principal theodicies share a common strategy: they identify a good that is served by the suffering in question and argue that an omnipotent, omnibenevolent God permits the suffering for the sake of that good. The free-will theodicy identifies the value of moral agency. The soul-making theodicy identifies the development of character. The greater-good theodicy identifies a providential plan whose full goodness exceeds our present comprehension. In each case, the suffering is connected to a recognisable good that is held to justify its permission.

One might codify this strategy in the following definition: the existence of suffering is compatible with divine omnipotence and omnibenevolence if and only if the suffering serves a morally sufficient reason or greater good, an omnipotent God could not have achieved that good without permitting the suffering, and the good outweighs the suffering. This definition captures the logical structure of the theodicies. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is the suffering of a very young infant who dies of a painful congenital disease within days of birth. The infant has exercised no free will and made no moral choice, so the free-will defence does not apply. The infant has had no opportunity for moral or spiritual growth, so the soul-making defence does not apply. One may invoke the greater-good defence and assert that the suffering is a necessary component of a larger plan. But one should hesitate to accept this claim when the sufferer is an infant who derives no benefit whatever from the suffering and possesses no capacity for the goods that the theodicies characteristically invoke. It appears that the definition, by requiring only that the suffering serve some greater good without specifying that the good be related to the one who suffers, permits the justification of any suffering by appeal to goods entirely external to the sufferer.

background_cases: The cosmological argument from contingency argues that the existence of contingent beings requires an explanation, that this explanation cannot consist in an infinite regress of contingent beings, and that therefore there must exist a necessary being — God — whose existence is self-explanatory; the argument treats the existence of the world as a datum requiring explanation and infers the existence of God as the best or only explanation.

The teleological argument argues that the order and apparent design of the natural world — the intricate adaptation of organisms to their environments, the fine-tuning of physical constants — points to an intelligent designer; the argument treats the orderliness of nature as a datum and infers divine intelligence as the best explanation.

The moral argument argues that the existence of objective moral obligations — the unconditional character of the demand to be just, to be truthful — requires a ground that transcends the natural world, and infers the existence of God as the source of moral authority; the argument treats moral experience as a datum and infers a transcendent ground.
definition: An argument for the existence of God is rationally compelling if and only if (i) its premises are more probable than not, (ii) its conclusion follows from its premises by valid deductive or strong inductive reasoning, and (iii) there is no known sound argument whose conclusion contradicts the conclusion of the argument in question.
conditions: Its premises are more probable than not
Its conclusion follows from its premises by valid deductive or strong inductive reasoning
There is no known sound argument whose conclusion contradicts the conclusion of the argument in question
counterexample: Consider the teleological argument in its classical form. One might grant that its premises — the natural world exhibits order and apparent design — are more probable than not, and that the inference to an intelligent designer is inductively strong. Suppose further that no known sound argument establishes the non-existence of God. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the force of the argument was dramatically undermined by Darwin's theory of natural selection, which provided an alternative explanation for the order and apparent design of the biological world — an explanation that did not contradict the conclusion but rendered the inference to a designer unnecessary by supplying a rival account of the same data.
abductive_insight: The background cases reveal that arguments for the existence of God characteristically treat some feature of the world as a datum requiring explanation and infer God's existence as the best or only explanation. The definition captures the internal logical properties of such arguments but neglects the possibility that an argument's rational force may be undermined not by a contradicting conclusion but by the availability of an alternative explanation of the same data that makes the theistic inference superfluous.
passage:
The major arguments for the existence of God share a common structure: each treats some feature of the world — its contingency, its order, its moral fabric — as a datum requiring explanation and infers the existence of God as the best or only account. The cosmological argument treats the existence of contingent beings as its datum. The teleological argument treats the apparent design of nature. The moral argument treats the unconditional character of moral obligation. In each case, the theistic conclusion is reached by inference to the best explanation.

One might attempt to characterise a rationally compelling argument for God's existence as one whose premises are more probable than not, whose conclusion follows by valid deductive or strong inductive reasoning, and which faces no known sound argument for a contradictory conclusion. This characterisation captures the internal logical virtues of a sound argument. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with the teleological argument after Darwin. One may grant that the premises of the classical teleological argument — that nature exhibits order and apparent design — are more probable than not. One may grant that the inference to an intelligent designer is inductively strong. And one may concede that no known sound argument establishes the non-existence of God. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the rational force of the argument was profoundly undermined by Darwin's theory of natural selection, which provided a naturalistic explanation for the very order and design that the argument cited as its evidence. Darwin's theory does not contradict the theistic conclusion; it merely renders the inference to a designer unnecessary by supplying an alternative account of the same data. What the background cases reveal is that the rational force of an inference to the best explanation depends not only on its internal logic but on the absence of rival explanations. The definition, by requiring only that no contradicting conclusion be established, cannot detect the undermining effect of an alternative explanation that leaves the conclusion possible but superfluous.

background_cases: The parting of the Red Sea, as recounted in the Book of Exodus, is presented as an event in which God intervened to suspend the normal behaviour of water, allowing the Israelites to cross on dry ground; the miracle served the purpose of delivering a chosen people and was witnessed by an entire nation.

The healing miracles attributed to Jesus in the Gospels are presented as events in which disease was cured instantaneously by a word or a touch, in defiance of the normal course of pathology; the miracles served to manifest divine compassion and were witnessed by crowds of people.

The miracle of the sun at Fatima in 1917 is reported as an event in which the sun appeared to dance in the sky, witnessed by tens of thousands of people; the event was regarded as a divine authentication of the Marian apparitions that preceded it.
definition: An event E is a miracle if and only if (i) E cannot be explained by any known natural law, (ii) E is witnessed by multiple independent observers, and (iii) E is interpreted by its witnesses as an act of God.
conditions: E cannot be explained by any known natural law
E is witnessed by multiple independent observers
E is interpreted by its witnesses as an act of God
counterexample: Consider the spontaneous combustion of a chemical compound in a sealed laboratory, witnessed by multiple chemists who, finding no explanation in known chemistry, interpret the event as an act of God. The event cannot be explained by known natural law, it is witnessed by multiple independent observers, and it is interpreted as divine action. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that the event may simply be the result of an unknown chemical reaction — a gap in current scientific understanding rather than a suspension of natural law. Future chemistry may well provide the missing explanation.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a feature absent from the laboratory case: in each, the event occurred in a religious context — deliverance, healing, apparition — and was integrated into a larger narrative of divine purpose. The sealed laboratory event lacks this contextual embedding. The definition captures the negative criterion (inexplicability) and the social criterion (multiple witnesses) but omits the contextual criterion that distinguishes a miracle from a merely unexplained anomaly: genuine miracles, in the traditions that report them, occur within a framework of divine purpose and are intelligible as acts of a purposive agent.
passage:
The paradigmatic miracles of the religious traditions share features that go beyond mere inexplicability. The parting of the Red Sea occurred in the context of divine deliverance — it was intelligible as an act of a purposive agent delivering a chosen people. The healing miracles of Jesus occurred in the context of a ministry of compassion — they were intelligible as manifestations of divine love. The miracle of the sun at Fatima occurred in the context of Marian apparitions — it was intelligible as a divine authentication of a prophetic message. In each case, the event was not merely inexplicable but was embedded in a larger narrative of divine purpose.

One might attempt to define a miracle as an event that cannot be explained by known natural law, is witnessed by multiple independent observers, and is interpreted by its witnesses as an act of God. This definition captures the negative and social dimensions of miraculous events. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in a scientific laboratory. Suppose a chemical compound in a sealed vessel spontaneously combusts under conditions that no known law of chemistry can explain. Multiple chemists witness the event and, finding no natural explanation, interpret it as an act of God. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet one might observe that the event is far more likely a consequence of an unknown chemical reaction — a lacuna in current understanding — than a genuine miracle. What distinguishes the laboratory anomaly from the paradigmatic miracles is the absence of a religious context and a narrative of divine purpose. The background cases reveal that miracles are not merely inexplicable events but events that are intelligible as purposive acts within a framework of divine intention. The definition, by requiring only inexplicability, multiple witness, and theistic interpretation, cannot distinguish genuine miracles from unexplained anomalies that are misclassified by their observers.

background_cases: A surgeon who inflicts the pain of an incision upon a patient does so in order to remove a tumour that would otherwise prove fatal; the suffering is justified by its instrumental necessity for a greater good that could not be achieved without it.

A teacher who subjects a pupil to the frustration and difficulty of mathematical exercises does so in order to develop the pupil's reasoning capacities; the hardship is justified because the good of intellectual development could not be attained without the discipline of struggle.

A parent who allows a child to experience the disappointment of failure in a sporting contest does so because the development of resilience and character requires the experience of setback; the suffering is justified as a necessary condition for a good that the parent values more highly than the child's immediate comfort.
definition: The existence of suffering is compatible with the existence of an omnipotent and perfectly benevolent God if and only if (i) every instance of suffering serves as a necessary means to a greater good, (ii) the greater good in question could not have been achieved by any less painful means, and (iii) the sufferer or someone affected by the suffering is the beneficiary of the greater good.
conditions: Every instance of suffering serves as a necessary means to a greater good
The greater good in question could not have been achieved by any less painful means
The sufferer or someone affected by the suffering is the beneficiary of the greater good
counterexample: Consider the suffering of an infant who dies of a painful disease in a remote region, unwitnessed by anyone who might be moved to compassion or moral growth. No greater good accrues to the infant, who dies too young to have developed character. No greater good accrues to any witness, for there is none. The suffering serves no discernible instrumental purpose for anyone. All three conditions of the definition fail, yet the defender of divine benevolence may wish to maintain that God's purposes are inscrutable and that the suffering may serve a good beyond human comprehension.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the surgeon, the teacher, the parent — share a crucial feature: in each, the connection between suffering and greater good is transparent and intelligible to rational reflection. The infant's case lacks this transparency. The definition captures the logical structure of the instrumental justification of suffering but assumes that every instance of suffering must be intelligibly connected to a greater good. It cannot accommodate the theological response that some divine purposes may exceed human comprehension, nor can it account for suffering that appears, by every available measure, to serve no good whatever.
passage:
When one examines the most persuasive justifications of suffering, a common structure emerges. The surgeon who inflicts pain to remove a tumour, the teacher who imposes the discipline of difficult exercises, and the parent who permits the disappointment of failure all justify suffering as a necessary means to a greater good. In each case, the connection between the suffering and the good is transparent: we can see why the pain is necessary and who benefits from it.

One might attempt to reconcile the existence of suffering with the existence of an omnipotent and perfectly benevolent God by requiring that every instance of suffering serve as a necessary means to a greater good, that the good could not be achieved by less painful means, and that the sufferer or someone affected is the beneficiary. This framework captures the instrumental logic of the background cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with the suffering of an infant who dies of a painful disease in a remote and uninhabited region, with no witness to be moved to compassion or moral reflection. No greater good accrues to the infant, who perishes before developing the capacities that suffering might cultivate. No greater good accrues to any observer, for there is none. The suffering appears, by every available criterion, to serve no purpose whatever. The definition's three conditions all fail. Yet the defender of divine benevolence may respond that God's purposes exceed human comprehension — that the suffering serves a good we cannot discern. What the background cases reveal, and the infant's case challenges, is the assumption that the connection between suffering and greater good must be intelligible to human reason. The definition demands this intelligibility but cannot accommodate the theological insistence that some divine purposes lie beyond the reach of finite understanding.

background_cases: The cosmological argument, in its most careful formulations, proceeds from the premise that contingent beings exist to the conclusion that a necessary being — identified with a deity — must exist as the ultimate ground of contingent existence; the argument's force depends upon the principle that the existence of contingent beings requires an explanation that cannot itself be contingent.

The teleological argument, in its classical form, proceeds from the observation of order and purpose in nature — the adaptation of organisms to their environments, the mathematical regularity of physical laws — to the conclusion that a designing intelligence is responsible; the argument's force depends upon the implausibility of attributing complex order to unguided processes.

The moral argument proceeds from the existence of objective moral obligations to the conclusion that a moral lawgiver must exist; the argument's force depends upon the claim that objective moral facts require a ground in a personal being whose commands constitute moral law.
definition: An argument A for the existence of a supreme being is rationally compelling if and only if (i) A's premises are more plausible than their negations, (ii) A's inferences are valid, and (iii) A's conclusion is not subject to an equally plausible alternative explanation.
conditions: A's premises are more plausible than their negations
A's inferences are valid
A's conclusion is not subject to an equally plausible alternative explanation
counterexample: Consider a version of the teleological argument that observes the remarkable fine-tuning of the physical constants of the universe — if any of several constants were slightly different, life as we understand it could not exist — and infers that a supreme intelligence calibrated these constants for the purpose of producing life. The premises, one might argue, are more plausible than their negations: the constants are indeed finely tuned, and the existence of life does depend upon their precise values. The inference is valid. Yet the conclusion is subject to an equally plausible alternative: the multiverse hypothesis, which holds that a vast number of universes exist with different constants, and that we necessarily find ourselves in one compatible with life. The third condition is not met, yet this failure reveals a deeper difficulty — the definition cannot adjudicate between the theistic and multiverse explanations because it provides no criterion for weighing explanatory simplicity, scope, or coherence.
abductive_insight: The background cases reveal that the most compelling arguments for the existence of a supreme being succeed not merely by meeting formal conditions of plausibility and validity but by offering an explanation that is simpler, more unified, and more coherent with our broader understanding than any available alternative. The cosmological argument's force depends upon the claim that a necessary being is a simpler and more satisfying stopping point than an infinite regress. The teleological argument's force depends upon the claim that intelligent design is a more unified explanation than brute coincidence. The definition captures the formal features of rational compulsion but omits the comparative explanatory virtues — simplicity, unity, coherence with background understanding — that determine whether an argument is genuinely compelling in the face of alternatives.
passage:
The arguments for the existence of a supreme being that have most exercised philosophical attention share a structural feature beyond formal validity and plausible premises. The cosmological argument derives its force from the claim that a necessary being provides a simpler and more satisfying terminus to the chain of explanation than an infinite regress of contingent causes. The teleological argument derives its force from the claim that intelligent design offers a more unified account of natural order than the supposition of unguided coincidence. The moral argument derives its force from the claim that a personal moral lawgiver provides a more coherent ground for objective moral obligations than any impersonal alternative. In each case, the argument's compulsion depends upon comparative explanatory virtues.

One might attempt to characterise a rationally compelling argument for the existence of a supreme being as one whose premises are more plausible than their negations, whose inferences are valid, and whose conclusion is not subject to an equally plausible alternative explanation. This definition captures the formal desiderata of rational compulsion. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with the argument from fine-tuning. The physical constants of the universe appear finely tuned for the existence of life; small variations would render life impossible. The premises are plausible. The inference to a designing intelligence is valid. Yet the conclusion faces an equally plausible alternative: the multiverse hypothesis, which holds that vastly many universes exist with varying constants, and that we necessarily inhabit one compatible with life. The third condition fails. But the definition provides no resources for adjudicating between these alternatives. What the background cases reveal is that the compelling force of theistic arguments depends upon comparative explanatory virtues — simplicity, unity, coherence — that the definition does not capture. The definition tells us that an argument fails when an equally plausible alternative exists, but it cannot tell us which alternative is preferable, because it lacks the criteria — parsimony, unifying power, coherence with broader understanding — by which explanatory rivals are evaluated.

background_cases: The parting of the Red Sea in the Exodus narrative functions as a miracle not merely because it violates natural law but because it occurs at the precise moment when the Israelites face destruction, thereby serving as a sign of divine deliverance within a narrative of covenantal faithfulness; the event's miraculous character is inseparable from its narrative context.

The healing miracles attributed to Jesus in the Gospels function as signs not merely because they involve the restoration of health but because each healing occurs in a context that reveals something about the character and purposes of the divine — compassion for the suffering, inclusion of the marginalised, the inauguration of a new order; the miracles are embedded in a web of theological significance.

The miracle of the burning bush in Exodus functions as a sign not merely because a bush burns without being consumed but because the event occurs in the context of Moses' call to prophetic leadership, serving as a vehicle for divine self-disclosure and the commissioning of a liberator; removed from this context, an unconsumed bush would be merely an anomaly.
definition: An event E constitutes a miracle if and only if (i) E is an event that cannot be explained by the known laws of nature, (ii) E is witnessed by multiple credible observers, and (iii) E produces a sense of awe or wonder in those who witness it.
conditions: E is an event that cannot be explained by the known laws of nature
E is witnessed by multiple credible observers
E produces a sense of awe or wonder in those who witness it
counterexample: Consider an unexplained atmospheric phenomenon — a sustained display of ball lightning of unusual duration and brilliance — witnessed by multiple credible observers in an open field. The phenomenon cannot be explained by the known laws of atmospheric physics. Multiple credible observers are present and attest to it. And it produces a profound sense of awe and wonder. All three conditions are met. Yet the observers are meteorologists attending an outdoor conference, and none regards the event as a miracle — merely as an unexplained natural phenomenon awaiting scientific elucidation. The event is anomalous, awe-inspiring, and well-attested, but it is not a miracle.
abductive_insight: The background cases reveal that the events traditionally regarded as miracles possess a feature entirely absent from the ball-lightning case: they are embedded in a religious narrative that endows them with significance as signs of divine action. The parting of the Red Sea is a miracle because it occurs at the moment of Israel's deliverance. The Gospel healings are miracles because they reveal divine compassion and inaugurate a new order. The burning bush is a miracle because it serves as the vehicle of divine self-disclosure. Removed from these narrative contexts, each event would be merely anomalous. The definition captures the phenomenological and evidential features of miracles — inexplicability, attestation, awe — but omits the narrative and theological context that transforms an anomaly into a sign.

When one examines the events traditionally regarded as miracles within the great religious traditions, a structural feature emerges that goes beyond mere inexplicability and wonder. The parting of the Red Sea is not merely an unexplained hydrological event; it occurs at the precise moment when the Israelites face annihilation, and it functions within the Exodus narrative as a sign of covenantal deliverance. The healing miracles of the Gospels are not merely unexplained medical recoveries; each occurs in a context that reveals divine compassion and announces the inauguration of a transformed order. The burning bush is not merely an anomalous combustion; it serves as the vehicle of divine self-disclosure and the commissioning of a prophetic leader. In each case, the event's status as a miracle is inseparable from the narrative in which it is embedded.

One might attempt to characterise a miracle as an event that cannot be explained by the known laws of nature, is witnessed by multiple credible observers, and produces a sense of awe or wonder. This definition captures the phenomenological and evidential features commonly associated with miracles. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with an unexplained atmospheric phenomenon. Suppose a sustained display of ball lightning of unusual duration and brilliance appears over an open field, witnessed by multiple credible observers — meteorologists attending a conference. The phenomenon cannot be explained by known atmospheric physics. The witnesses are numerous and credible. And the display produces a profound sense of awe. All three conditions are met. Yet none of the observers regards the event as a miracle; they regard it as an unexplained natural phenomenon awaiting scientific analysis. What the background cases possess and the ball-lightning case lacks is a narrative and theological context that transforms the inexplicable into a sign. The definition, by attending only to inexplicability, attestation, and wonder, cannot distinguish a miracle from a mere anomaly.

background_cases: Abraham's willingness to sacrifice Isaac, as recounted in Genesis, exemplifies faith not merely as the acceptance of a proposition but as a radical act of trust and obedience toward a being whose purposes remain inscrutable; Abraham does not understand why the sacrifice is demanded, yet he acts in the confidence that the demand is ultimately intelligible, even if not to him.

The faith of Job, who maintains his integrity and his trust in the divine despite the destruction of his family, his wealth, and his health, exemplifies faith not as the belief that things will improve but as a sustained orientation of trust toward a being whose justice is not manifest in the present circumstances; Job's faith persists precisely in the absence of any visible ground for it.

Kierkegaard's analysis of the 'leap of faith' characterises faith not as the acceptance of propositions for which evidence is insufficient but as an existential commitment that engages the whole person — a commitment made not because the evidence supports it but because the alternative — a life of aesthetic or merely ethical existence — fails to address the deepest dimensions of human concern.
definition: A person S possesses genuine faith if and only if (i) S assents to certain propositions concerning a transcendent reality, (ii) S's assent is maintained despite the absence of demonstrative proof, and (iii) S's assent is the product of deliberation rather than mere habit or social pressure.
conditions: S assents to certain propositions concerning a transcendent reality
S's assent is maintained despite the absence of demonstrative proof
S's assent is the product of deliberation rather than mere habit or social pressure
counterexample: Consider a philosopher who, after careful deliberation, assents to the proposition that a transcendent reality exists, maintains this assent despite the absence of demonstrative proof, and does so as the product of genuine philosophical reflection rather than habit or social pressure. The philosopher regards the proposition as the best available hypothesis given the totality of philosophical argument, but remains emotionally detached from it — prepared to abandon it the moment a superior argument presents itself. All three conditions are met. Yet we should hesitate to attribute genuine faith to this philosopher, for the assent is purely intellectual, carrying no personal risk, no existential commitment, and no transformation of the philosopher's way of living.
abductive_insight: The background cases reveal that genuine faith, as exemplified by Abraham, Job, and Kierkegaard's analysis, involves not merely propositional assent maintained under uncertainty but an existential commitment that engages the whole person — a willingness to act, to suffer, to reorder one's life in accordance with one's relationship to the transcendent. Abraham's faith involved the willingness to sacrifice his son. Job's faith involved the sustained orientation of his whole person toward the divine despite manifest suffering. Kierkegaard's 'leap' involves the staking of one's existence upon a commitment that cannot be underwritten by reason. The philosopher's detached assent, though deliberate and maintained under uncertainty, lacks this existential dimension entirely. The definition captures the cognitive features of faith but omits the dimension of personal commitment and existential risk that distinguishes faith from mere intellectual assent to a well-considered hypothesis.
passage:
When one examines the figures most commonly cited as exemplars of faith — Abraham, Job, the saints and mystics of the great religious traditions — a feature emerges that goes beyond the mere acceptance of propositions under conditions of uncertainty. Abraham's willingness to sacrifice Isaac is an act of faith not because Abraham accepts a proposition without proof but because he commits his entire existence — his deepest attachment, his son — to a trust whose grounds he cannot articulate. Job's faith is exemplary not because he believes certain propositions about the divine but because he maintains a sustained orientation of trust toward a being whose purposes remain opaque in the face of devastating suffering. Kierkegaard's analysis of the 'leap of faith' characterises faith not as propositional assent but as an existential commitment that engages the whole person and cannot be underwritten by philosophical argument.

One might attempt to characterise genuine faith as the deliberate assent to propositions concerning a transcendent reality, maintained despite the absence of demonstrative proof. This definition captures the cognitive features commonly associated with faith: propositional content, maintenance under uncertainty, and deliberateness. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with a detached philosopher who, after careful reflection, assents to the proposition that a transcendent reality exists. The philosopher maintains this assent despite the absence of proof and does so as the product of genuine deliberation. All three conditions are met. Yet the philosopher remains emotionally and existentially detached from this conclusion — prepared to abandon it upon encountering a superior argument, and in no way transformed by it. We should hesitate to attribute genuine faith to this philosopher. What the background cases possess and the philosopher's detached assent lacks is the dimension of personal commitment and existential risk — the willingness to stake one's life, one's actions, and one's orientation in the world upon a relationship to the transcendent that cannot be secured by argument. The definition, by attending only to propositional assent and its maintenance under uncertainty, cannot distinguish faith from mere intellectual opinion.

background_cases: The cosmological argument, in its most rigorous formulations, proceeds from the existence of contingent beings to the necessity of a being whose existence is not contingent upon anything else; the argument's force depends upon the premise that an infinite regress of contingent causes is impossible — a premise whose truth is contested but whose role in the argument is clearly identifiable and open to scrutiny.

The teleological argument, in its classical form, proceeds from the observed order and apparent purposiveness of natural phenomena to the existence of an intelligent designer; the argument's force depends upon the premise that order and purposiveness cannot arise from unguided natural processes — a premise whose status has been radically altered by the development of evolutionary theory.

The moral argument proceeds from the existence of objective moral obligations to the existence of a being who grounds those obligations; the argument's force depends upon the premise that objective moral obligations cannot exist without a transcendent guarantor — a premise that many moral philosophers have challenged on independent grounds.
definition: An argument A constitutes a compelling argument for the existence of a divine being if and only if (i) A's premises are more plausible than their negations, (ii) A's logical form is valid, and (iii) A's conclusion is not established by any of the premises taken individually but only by their conjunction.
conditions: A's premises are more plausible than their negations
A's logical form is valid
A's conclusion is not established by any of the premises taken individually but only by their conjunction
counterexample: Consider an argument whose first premise states that whatever begins to exist has a cause, whose second premise states that the universe began to exist, and whose conclusion states that the universe has a cause that is a divine being. Each premise may be more plausible than its negation. The logical form is valid. The conclusion follows from the conjunction of the premises, not from either alone. All three conditions are met. Yet the conclusion asserts more than the premises warrant: the premises establish at most that the universe has a cause, not that this cause possesses the attributes of a divine being — omnipotence, consciousness, goodness. The gap between the conclusion the premises support and the conclusion the argument asserts is not bridged by the definition.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is the presence of a clearly identifiable and independently assessable premise whose acceptance or rejection determines the argument's force. The definition fails to require that the conclusion not exceed what the premises jointly warrant — that it assert only what the premises, taken together, logically establish, rather than supplementing their logical force with unstated assumptions.
passage:
The major arguments for the existence of a divine being exhibit a common structural feature that merits attention. The cosmological argument depends upon the premise that an infinite regress of contingent causes is impossible — a premise whose truth is contested but clearly identifiable. The teleological argument depends upon the premise that order cannot arise without intelligent design — a premise whose standing has been transformed by evolutionary biology. The moral argument depends upon the premise that objective moral obligations require a transcendent guarantor — a premise challenged by secular moral philosophy. In each case, the argument's force is concentrated in a specific, identifiable premise that can be independently assessed. The strength of the argument rises or falls with the plausibility of that premise.

One might attempt to define a compelling argument for the existence of a divine being as one whose premises are individually more plausible than their negations, whose logical form is valid, and whose conclusion is established by the conjunction of the premises rather than by any single premise alone. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be constructed. Consider an argument from two premises: whatever begins to exist has a cause; the universe began to exist. Each premise may be more plausible than its negation. The form is valid. The conclusion — that the universe has a cause — follows from their conjunction. All three conditions are met. Yet the argument's proponent typically draws a further conclusion: that this cause is a divine being possessing omnipotence, consciousness, and moral perfection. This further conclusion far exceeds what the premises warrant; they establish at most the existence of a cause, not its nature. One might observe that what the definition fails to require is that the conclusion not exceed the logical warrant of the premises — that the argument assert only what the premises jointly establish, without tacitly supplementing their force with unstated assumptions about the nature of ultimate causes.

background_cases: When Kierkegaard characterised faith as a leap beyond the deliverances of reason, he was describing not a deficiency in the believer's reasoning but a distinctive mode of engagement — a willingness to commit oneself to that which reason alone cannot secure; the leap was not irrational but supra-rational, involving the whole person rather than the intellect alone.

When Pascal argued that the heart has its reasons which reason does not know, he was pointing to a mode of apprehension that operates alongside discursive reasoning — an intuitive grasp of truths that cannot be demonstrated by logical argument yet are not therefore arbitrary or groundless; faith, on this view, involves a faculty distinct from but not opposed to reason.

When Newman distinguished between notional assent and real assent, he was arguing that faith involves not merely the intellectual acceptance of propositions but a lived, personal engagement with their content — a commitment that transforms conduct and character in ways that notional assent alone does not.
definition: A person S has genuine faith if and only if (i) S accepts certain propositions as true, (ii) S's acceptance is not based upon demonstrative proof, and (iii) S maintains this acceptance steadfastly over time.

S's acceptance is not based upon demonstrative proof
S maintains this acceptance steadfastly over time

All three conditions are met. Yet this is not faith in any sense that Kierkegaard, Pascal, or Newman would recognise. It is a speculative conviction about an empirical matter, lacking the existential depth, the personal transformation, and the orientation toward that which transcends the empirical that characterise what these thinkers meant by faith.

The definition captures the propositional and evidential dimensions of faith but omits this existential and transformative dimension.

background_cases: When the Israelites were reported to have crossed the Red Sea on dry ground, the event was regarded as miraculous not merely because it was extraordinary but because it occurred at a moment of supreme historical and religious significance — the liberation of a people from bondage; the timing and context were essential to its miraculous character.

When the healing miracles attributed to Christ in the Gospels were recorded, they were regarded as miraculous not merely because they involved recoveries that defied medical expectation but because they occurred within a context of teaching and proclamation — the healings were signs pointing beyond themselves to a larger reality; their significance was inseparable from their context.

When the miracle of the sun at Fatima was reported in 1917, those who regarded it as miraculous did so not merely because the solar phenomenon was extraordinary but because it occurred in response to a specific prediction, at a specific place, before a specific audience, and within the context of a series of apparitions; the event's miraculous character was constituted by the convergence of these contextual factors.
definition: An event E is a miracle if and only if (i) E cannot be explained by any known natural law, (ii) E is witnessed by multiple credible observers, and (iii) E is subsequently attested in a reliable historical record.

E is witnessed by multiple credible observers

At the time of observation, the precise trajectory and composition of the meteorite might resist explanation by known laws — the event might be, in the relevant sense, anomalous. All three conditions are met. Yet no one would call the impact a miracle. It is a natural event whose full explanation awaits further investigation, not an event whose significance transcends the natural order.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the event's miraculous character is constituted not merely by its inexplicability and attestation but by its occurrence within a context of religious significance — at a moment, in a setting, and in relation to a narrative that endows the event with meaning beyond its physical description. The definition fails to require this contextual dimension, treating miracles as merely unexplained events rather than events whose significance is constituted by the context in which they occur.

background_cases: When the biblical narrative recounts the parting of the Red Sea, the event is presented as miraculous not merely because it violates the ordinary course of nature but because it occurs at a moment of supreme significance — the liberation of the Israelites from slavery — and is attributed to divine agency acting with a specific redemptive purpose; the miracle is intelligible only within the theological narrative of deliverance.

background_cases: When Abraham, in the biblical narrative, trusts the promise that his descendants will be as numerous as the stars despite his advanced age and Sarah's barrenness, the faith exemplified is not merely the acceptance of an improbable proposition but a personal trust in the character and reliability of the one who makes the promise — a relational attitude directed toward a being, not merely an assent to a claim.

When the disciples in the Gospel narratives are said to have faith, the attitude described is not merely the belief that certain propositions are true but a personal commitment to following a teacher in whom they place their trust — a trust that involves vulnerability, loyalty, and the willingness to act on the basis of a relationship rather than a demonstration.

When Kierkegaard describes the leap of faith, he characterises it not as the mere acceptance of an uncertain proposition but as a passionate personal commitment that engages the whole person — a venture that cannot be reduced to the endorsement of a claim, however uncertain, because it involves the transformation of the self in relation to the absolute.

The available evidence neither conclusively establishes nor conclusively refutes P
S maintains acceptance of P despite the possibility of its falsehood
counterexample: Consider a gambler who accepts as true the proposition that his horse will win the next race. The available evidence neither conclusively establishes nor refutes this — the horse has a plausible chance but no certainty. The gambler maintains his acceptance despite the possibility that the horse will lose. All three conditions are met. Yet the gambler's confidence in his horse is not faith in any philosophically or religiously significant sense. It is a wager, an optimistic prediction, or perhaps a compulsion — but it lacks the dimension of personal trust, moral commitment, and existential engagement that the concept of faith is ordinarily taken to involve.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that faith involves not merely the propositional acceptance of an uncertain claim but a personal, relational trust directed toward a being or a reality — a trust that engages the whole person and involves commitment, vulnerability, and existential transformation. background_cases: When Leibniz argued that this is the best of all possible worlds, his defence of divine benevolence rested on the claim that the evils we observe are necessary components of a maximally good whole — that the suffering of individuals contributes to a cosmic optimum that could not be achieved without it; the defence required not merely that the suffering be compensated but that it be genuinely necessary for the realisation of the best possible totality.

When Augustine explained suffering by appeal to the fall of man, the defence succeeded within its framework because it traced suffering to a specific cause — the misuse of free will by created beings — that was distinct from divine intention; the suffering was a consequence of creaturely choice, not a product of divine malice or indifference.

When Irenaeus proposed that suffering serves the purpose of soul-making — that moral and spiritual growth require the experience of adversity — the defence derived its force from the identification of a specific good that suffering serves and that could not, by the nature of the case, be achieved without it; mere compensation after the fact would not have been sufficient, because the good in question required the experience of suffering as its condition.
definition: The existence of suffering is compatible with the existence of an omnipotent and perfectly good deity if and only if (i) every instance of suffering is such that its occurrence is permitted by the deity for the sake of a greater good, (ii) the greater good could not be achieved without permitting the suffering, and (iii) the suffering is proportionate to the good it serves.

The greater good could not be achieved without permitting the suffering
The suffering is proportionate to the good it serves
counterexample: Consider the suffering of an animal — say, a fawn trapped in a forest fire — that dies in prolonged agony over the course of several days, unobserved by any human being. The fawn's suffering cannot serve the purpose of soul-making, since the fawn is not a moral agent. It cannot be traced to a misuse of free will. It is not observed by anyone who might draw a moral lesson from it. To claim that this suffering is permitted for the sake of a greater good, that the good requires it, and that it is proportionate, one must posit a good so remote from human understanding that the claim becomes unfalsifiable — it is no longer a defence but an expression of trust that a defence exists.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the good served by suffering is specifiable and connected to the nature of the beings who suffer — moral growth for moral agents, the realisation of a maximally good whole, the consequences of free choice. The definition fails when confronted with suffering that cannot be connected to any specifiable good, revealing that the conditions are either unsatisfiable in certain cases or can be maintained only by rendering the notion of a greater good so elastic as to deprive it of content.

The most influential defences of divine benevolence in the face of suffering share a common structural feature. Leibniz argued that the evils of the world are necessary components of the best possible totality — each instance of suffering contributes to a cosmic optimum unattainable without it. Augustine traced suffering to the misuse of free will by created beings, locating the cause in creaturely choice rather than divine intention. Irenaeus proposed that suffering serves the purpose of moral and spiritual development — that growth of character requires the experience of adversity. In each case, the defence identifies a specific good that suffering serves and argues that the good could not, in the nature of the case, be realised without the suffering.

These cases invite the formulation of a general principle: suffering is compatible with divine benevolence when every instance is permitted for the sake of a greater good that requires it and is proportionate to it. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the natural world. Consider a fawn trapped in a forest fire, dying over the course of several days in unrelieved agony, entirely unobserved by any human being. The fawn is not a moral agent; its suffering cannot serve the end of soul-making. It has not exercised free will. No human observer draws a moral lesson from it. To maintain that this suffering is permitted for a greater good that requires it and is proportionate to it, one must posit a good so remote from anything specifiable that the claim becomes immune to assessment. One might observe that what the background cases share — and what the definition cannot accommodate in this instance — is that the good served by suffering is identifiable and connected to the nature of the beings who suffer. When no such connection can be made, the conditions are either unsatisfied or satisfied only by evacuating the concept of a greater good of all determinate content.

background_cases: When Leibniz argued that this is the best of all possible worlds, his theodicy acknowledged that every category of suffering — natural disasters, human cruelty, disease, the suffering of the innocent — must be accounted for, not merely those forms of suffering that most readily admit of justification; the theodicy's ambition was comprehensive.

When Irenaeus proposed that the world is a vale of soul-making designed to develop moral character, his account was taken seriously precisely insofar as it aspired to cover the full range of suffering — though critics noted its difficulty in accounting for the suffering of those incapable of moral growth, such as infants and animals.

When Augustine attributed evil to the free choices of created beings who turned away from the good, the account was powerful insofar as it explained moral evil comprehensively, but was recognised as incomplete because natural evils — earthquakes, plagues, droughts — could not obviously be traced to the misuse of creaturely freedom.
definition: A theodicy T is adequate if and only if (i) T identifies a good G that could not be achieved without permitting some suffering, (ii) the good G is of sufficient magnitude to outweigh the suffering permitted, and (iii) T is internally consistent.
conditions: T identifies a good G that could not be achieved without permitting some suffering
The good G is of sufficient magnitude to outweigh the suffering permitted
T is internally consistent
counterexample: Consider a theodicy that identifies the good of aesthetic variety — the claim that a world containing both joy and suffering is more beautiful than a uniformly happy world, and that this aesthetic richness could not be achieved without suffering. Suppose the good of aesthetic variety is held to outweigh the suffering it requires, and the account is internally consistent. All three conditions are met. Yet the theodicy is inadequate, for it treats the suffering of sentient beings as a means to an aesthetic end — it justifies the agony of a dying child by appeal to the beauty of a varied cosmos, which seems to display a fundamental indifference to the moral significance of the suffering it purports to justify.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is the recognition that an adequate theodicy must not merely identify a compensating good but must show that the good bears a morally appropriate relationship to the suffering — that the sufferers are not treated merely as instruments for the achievement of an end in which their suffering plays no constructive role for them. The definition, by requiring only that a sufficiently great good necessitate the suffering and that the account be consistent, cannot distinguish theodicies that respect the moral significance of the living with those that exploit it as a mere means to an unrelated end.

When Irenaeus proposed the soul-making theodicy, the account was taken seriously insofar as it endeavoured to connect suffering to the moral development of the sufferer. One might attempt to capture the requirements of an adequate theodicy by demanding that it identify a good that could not be achieved without some suffering, that the good be of sufficient magnitude to outweigh the suffering, and that the account be internally consistent. Suppose the aesthetic good is held to be of sufficient magnitude to outweigh all suffering, and the account is internally consistent. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that this theodicy is deeply inadequate. It treats the suffering of sentient beings — the agony of a dying child, the torment of a victim of natural disaster — as a means to an aesthetic end. The sufferers derive no benefit from the beauty their suffering creates. What the background cases reveal — and what the definition fails to require — is that the compensating good bear a morally appropriate relationship to the suffering: that the sufferers not be treated merely as instruments for the achievement of an end to which their own suffering is irrelevant.

background_cases: When the parting of the Red Sea is cited as a miracle in the Judaeo-Christian tradition, the event is regarded as miraculous not merely because it appeared to violate natural law but because its occurrence at the precise moment of the Israelites' need, and its role in the larger narrative of divine deliverance, suggested a purposive connection between the event and a divine plan.

When the healing miracles attributed to various saints are cited, the events are taken to be miraculous not merely because they involved apparent violations of medical regularity but because the healings occurred in contexts of prayer, devotion, and moral transformation that connected them to a larger religious narrative.

When the miracle of the sun at Fatima is cited, the event is regarded as miraculous not merely because of the unusual solar phenomenon reported by witnesses but because of the religious context in which it occurred — a context of prophetic announcement, public prayer, and devotional expectation that connected the phenomenon to a specific divine communication.
definition: An event E constitutes a miracle if and only if (i) E cannot be explained by any known natural law, (ii) E occurs in a context where a divine agent has been invoked, and (iii) E is reported by multiple independent witnesses.
conditions: E cannot be explained by any known natural law
E occurs in a context where a divine agent has been invoked
E is reported by multiple independent witnesses

The phenomenon cannot be explained by any natural law known to the sailors. It occurs in a context where a divine agent has been invoked. It is witnessed independently by every member of the crew. All three conditions are met. Yet the phenomenon is Saint Elmo's fire — a well-understood atmospheric electrical discharge that, though unknown to the sailors, is entirely natural. The event's inexplicability reflects the sailors' ignorance, not a genuine suspension of natural order.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the event is not merely unexplained by known natural law but is such that its connection to the religious context is best accounted for by purposive divine action rather than by natural causes unknown to the witnesses. The definition, by requiring only that the event be unexplained by known law, cannot distinguish genuinely miraculous events from natural phenomena that are merely unfamiliar to the observers.

background_cases: When Abraham is cited as a paradigm of faith for his willingness to sacrifice Isaac at divine command, the characterisation reflects not merely that Abraham acted without conclusive evidence but that his commitment persisted in the face of a demand that contradicted his deepest moral convictions and natural affections — his faith involved a willingness to sustain commitment through the most severe existential trial.

When the early Christian martyrs are cited as exemplars of faith, the characterisation acknowledges not merely their acceptance of unproven propositions but their willingness to endure suffering, persecution, and death rather than abandon their commitments — their faith was tested and sustained through extreme adversity.

When Kierkegaard characterised the knight of faith as one who makes a leap beyond the deliverances of reason, the emphasis fell not on the mere absence of evidence but on the existential risk involved — the willingness to stake one's entire existence upon a commitment that reason alone cannot secure.

S acts in accordance with P in daily life
S maintains acceptance of P over a sustained period

She acts in accordance with this acceptance by purchasing and consuming the cereal daily. She maintains this acceptance over many years. All three conditions are met. Yet we should not describe this person as having faith in the cereal. Her acceptance is a matter of consumer habit, entirely trivial in its existential significance, and it has never been tested against any serious challenge or adversity.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that faith involves not merely the sustained acceptance of an unproven proposition and action in accordance with it, but a willingness to maintain that commitment in the face of serious existential challenge, adversity, or trial — circumstances in which the cost of maintaining the commitment is substantial and the temptation to abandon it is real. The definition, by requiring only acceptance, action, and temporal persistence, cannot distinguish faith from any trivial habit of acceptance that has simply never been tested.

background_cases: When Aquinas argued from the existence of contingent beings to a necessary being, the argument's force depended not merely on the logical structure of the inference but on the assumption that the concept of a necessary being is coherent — that there could be a being whose non-existence is impossible — and that the conclusion refers to an actual entity rather than to a logical construct.

When Anselm argued that the greatest conceivable being must exist because existence is a perfection, the argument's persuasiveness hinged on whether existence can properly be treated as a predicate that adds to a thing's perfection — a question about the logical grammar of 'existence' rather than about the formal validity of the deduction.

When Leibniz argued from the principle of sufficient reason to the existence of a being that is its own sufficient reason, the argument's success depended on whether the principle of sufficient reason is itself self-evidently true or merely a methodological postulate, and on whether the concept of a self-sufficient being is coherent rather than merely formally well-formed.
definition: An argument A for the existence of a divine being is sound if and only if (i) the premises of A are true, (ii) the inference from the premises to the conclusion is deductively valid, and (iii) the conclusion of A asserts the existence of a being possessing the attributes traditionally associated with divinity.
conditions: The premises of A are true
The inference from the premises to the conclusion is deductively valid
The conclusion of A asserts the existence of a being possessing the attributes traditionally associated with divinity

The inference is deductively valid. Let us grant the truth of both premises. The conclusion asserts the existence of a being possessing traditional divine attributes. All three conditions are met. Yet the soundness of the argument depends entirely on the second premise — the coherence of the concept — which is precisely what is at issue. The argument is logically valid and, if its premises are granted, sound, yet it establishes nothing, for the controversial step is smuggled into a premise that cannot be assessed independently of the conclusion.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the assessment of soundness involves not merely the truth of premises and the validity of inference but the independent assessability of the crucial premises — whether the key assumptions can be evaluated without already presupposing the truth of the conclusion. The definition fails to require that the premises of A be independently assessable, permitting arguments whose soundness is formally impeccable but whose persuasive force is nullified by the circularity or question-begging character of their premises.

background_cases: When Augustine proposed that evil is not a positive reality but a privation of good — a corruption or absence of what ought to be present — the theodicy derived its force from offering an account that applied uniformly: every instance of evil, from moral wickedness to natural suffering, could be understood as a deficiency rather than as a positive creation, thereby preserving the claim that a perfectly good creator need not be the author of evil.

When Plantinga proposed the free will defence — arguing that a world containing creatures who freely choose the good is more valuable than a world of automata, and that the possibility of freely choosing evil is the unavoidable cost of this greater good — the argument's power lay in its applicability to the full range of moral evil, since every instance of moral evil could be traced to the exercise of creaturely freedom.

When Irenaeus proposed that the world is a vale of soul-making in which suffering provides the necessary conditions for the development of virtue, the theodicy's appeal lay in its capacity to render a wide range of sufferings intelligible as conditions for moral and spiritual growth, thereby connecting each instance of suffering to a discernible purpose.
definition: A response R to the problem of evil is adequate if and only if (i) R provides a reason why a perfectly good, omnipotent, and omniscient being might permit evil, (ii) R does not deny any of the traditional attributes of the divine, and (iii) R is internally consistent.
conditions: R provides a reason why a perfectly good, omnipotent, and omniscient being might permit evil
R does not deny any of the traditional attributes of the divine
R is internally consistent
counterexample: Suppose someone argues that evil is permitted because the aesthetic beauty of the universe requires contrast — that suffering is to the cosmos what shadow is to a painting, a necessary element without which the overall composition would lack depth and interest. This provides a reason for the permission of evil. It does not deny any traditional divine attribute. And it is internally consistent. All three conditions are met. Yet the response is morally grotesque when applied to extreme cases: it implies that the suffering of innocent children is aesthetically justified, that their agony serves the same function as a shadow in a painting. The response, while internally consistent and formally adequate, fails to take seriously the moral weight of the suffering it purports to explain.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the proposed reason for the permission of evil bears a morally serious relation to the suffering it addresses — the reason treats the sufferer as a being whose experience matters, not as a mere element in an aesthetic or structural arrangement. The definition fails to require that R's proposed reason be morally commensurate with the severity and nature of the evil it is invoked to explain — that the reason treat the suffering of sentient beings with the seriousness that a morally adequate response demands.

background_cases: When the Gospel accounts report the resurrection of Lazarus, the event is presented not as an isolated anomaly but as an act whose significance is intelligible within a broader framework of divine purpose: the raising of Lazarus reveals the divine power over death and serves as a sign pointing to a specific theological truth about the relationship between the divine and mortal life.

When the Hebrew scriptures report the parting of the Red Sea, the event is presented as an act bearing a specific relation to the liberation of the Israelites from bondage — a purposive act embedded within a narrative of divine concern for a particular people. Its miraculous character is inseparable from its significance as an act of divine will directed toward a discernible end.

When hagiographic literature reports the miraculous healings performed by saints, the healings are presented as events whose occurrence is purposively related to the sanctity and mission of the healer — the miracle is not a random violation of natural order but an act whose occurrence and character are intelligible in light of a broader framework of divine purposes.
definition: An event M qualifies as a miracle if and only if (i) M is an event for which no natural explanation is currently available, (ii) M occurs in a context in which it is attributed to a divine agent, and (iii) M is attested by multiple independent witnesses.
conditions: M is an event for which no natural explanation is currently available
M occurs in a context in which it is attributed to a divine agent
M is attested by multiple independent witnesses
counterexample: Suppose multiple independent witnesses observe an unexplained phenomenon in a church — a statue that, without any detectable mechanism, rotates ninety degrees overnight. No natural explanation is currently available. The event occurs in a religious context and is attributed to divine agency. It is attested by the priest, the sacristan, and several parishioners who observed the statue's new position independently. All three conditions are met. Yet the rotation of a statue by ninety degrees, however inexplicable, lacks any discernible purpose or significance: it communicates nothing, heals no one, and bears no intelligible relation to any divine end. It is merely an unexplained anomaly in a religious setting.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the event identified as a miracle bears an intelligible purposive relation to a divine end — that it functions as a sign, an act of deliverance, or a manifestation of power whose occurrence is meaningful within a framework of divine agency and intention. The definition fails to require that M possess this purposive or significatory character, permitting any currently inexplicable anomaly in a religious setting to qualify as a miracle regardless of whether it bears any intelligible relation to divine purposes.

These cases invite a general characterisation: a miracle is an event for which no natural explanation is currently available, occurring in a context of divine attribution, and attested by multiple independent witnesses. One might observe that what the background cases share — and what the definition fails to require — is that the event bear an intelligible purposive relation to a divine end, functioning as a sign, an act of deliverance, or a manifestation of power whose occurrence is meaningful within a framework of divine intention.

background_cases: When the biblical narrative reports that the waters of the Red Sea parted to allow the Israelites to cross, the event was understood as miraculous because it involved the suspension of the natural behaviour of water in a manner that served a divine purpose: the deliverance of a people from bondage.

When the Gospel narratives report that Jesus walked upon the surface of the Sea of Galilee, the event was understood as miraculous because it involved a violation of the natural order — a human body being sustained upon water without sinking — and was interpreted as a sign of divine authority.

When hagiographic accounts report that a saint's body remained incorrupt after death, the event was understood as miraculous because it involved the suspension of the natural process of decomposition, signalling divine favour towards the deceased.
definition: An event E is miraculous if and only if (i) E involves a suspension or violation of the regular course of nature, (ii) E is witnessed or attested by credible persons, and (iii) E admits of no natural explanation.
conditions: E involves a suspension or violation of the regular course of nature
E is witnessed or attested by credible persons
E admits of no natural explanation
counterexample: Suppose a physician in the eighteenth century encounters a patient with a tumour that, by all medical understanding of the time, is invariably fatal. The tumour disappears completely without treatment. The disappearance is witnessed by multiple credible physicians. It involves a suspension of the regular course of nature as understood by contemporary medicine. And it admits of no natural explanation available at the time. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet suppose that two centuries later, medical science discovers that certain tumours are subject to spontaneous remission through immune-mediated mechanisms. The event, which once admitted of no natural explanation, now does. The miraculous status of the event proves to be a function of the state of scientific understanding at the time of its occurrence, not an intrinsic property of the event itself.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the events described were located within a theological framework that attributed them to divine agency independently of the state of natural science. The definition, by requiring that the event admit of no natural explanation, makes the miraculous status of an event hostage to the current state of scientific understanding, so that events judged miraculous at one time may be explained naturally at a later time. A satisfactory account of the miraculous, it appears, must specify the relation between the event and divine agency without reducing the miraculous to a gap in current scientific understanding.
