The paradigmatic miracles reported in the religious traditions of the West share a common feature: each involves a departure from the regular course of nature that is interpreted as a sign or act of divine power. The parting of the Red Sea, the walking upon water, and the incorruption of a saint's body all involve the suspension of natural regularities in contexts understood as manifestations of divine agency.

These cases invite a general characterisation: a miraculous event involves a violation of the course of nature, is attested by credible witnesses, and admits of no natural explanation. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the history of medicine. Suppose an eighteenth-century physician encounters a patient with a tumour that, by the medical understanding of the period, is invariably fatal. The tumour disappears without treatment. The disappearance is witnessed by multiple credible physicians. It involves a departure from the regular course of nature as then understood. And it admits of no natural explanation available to contemporary medicine. All three conditions are met. Yet suppose that two centuries later, immunological research discovers that certain tumours are subject to spontaneous remission through well-characterised immune mechanisms. The event that once appeared to violate natural law is now explicable in natural terms. Its miraculous status proves to have been a function of the state of scientific understanding at the time, not an intrinsic feature of the event. What the background cases reveal — and what the definition fails to secure — is that genuine miracles are identified not merely by the absence of a natural explanation but by a positive attribution to divine agency that is independent of the current limits of scientific understanding.

background_cases: When Abraham, according to the biblical narrative, obeyed the command to sacrifice Isaac, his faith was understood to consist not in a mere belief about the future but in a radical act of trust and obedience that defied every expectation grounded in natural reason. The faith was volitional and existentially transformative.

When Pascal argued that it is rational to wager on the existence of the divine, the argument appealed not to evidence but to the practical consequences of belief and unbelief. Yet the resulting commitment, if genuinely adopted, was understood to involve a reorientation of the whole person — not merely an intellectual assent but a transformation of conduct and orientation.

When Kierkegaard described the 'leap of faith,' the metaphor was intended to convey that faith involves a movement of the will that goes beyond anything that rational deliberation can secure: a commitment made in the face of objective uncertainty and sustained by a passion that reason alone cannot generate.
definition: A person has faith if and only if (i) the person believes certain propositions for which conclusive evidence is lacking, (ii) the person's belief is sustained by an act of will rather than by evidence, and (iii) the person regards the believed propositions as of supreme importance to the conduct of life.
conditions: The person believes certain propositions for which conclusive evidence is lacking
The person's belief is sustained by an act of will rather than by evidence
The person regards the believed propositions as of supreme importance to the conduct of life
counterexample: Suppose a political ideologue believes that a particular economic system will inevitably triumph over all alternatives. Conclusive evidence for this proposition is lacking. The belief is sustained by an act of will — the ideologue commits to the proposition despite contrary evidence. And the ideologue regards this belief as of supreme importance to the conduct of his life, organising his activities, associations, and aspirations around it. All three conditions are met. Yet we should hesitate to describe this as faith in the sense that is of interest to the philosophy of religion, for the belief lacks the dimension of personal relationship or orientation towards a transcendent reality that characterises religious faith.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the faith described in each involves an orientation towards a reality that transcends the empirical — a divine command, a possible divine being, an absolute that exceeds rational comprehension. The definition, by requiring only will-sustained belief in propositions of supreme importance, fails to distinguish religious faith from ideological commitment, political fanaticism, or any other form of passionate conviction regarding unproven propositions. The missing element is that faith, in its religiously significant sense, involves an orientation towards a transcendent or ultimate reality.
passage:
The most instructive accounts of faith in the Western philosophical tradition share a structural feature that the most natural definitions of faith fail to capture. When Abraham obeyed the command to sacrifice Isaac, his faith consisted not merely in believing an unproven proposition but in a radical act of trust directed towards a transcendent divine command. When Pascal proposed his wager, the resulting commitment, if genuine, involved a reorientation of the whole person towards the possibility of a divine being. When Kierkegaard described the leap of faith, the metaphor conveyed a movement of the will directed towards an absolute that exceeds rational comprehension.

These cases invite a general characterisation: faith is the will-sustained belief in propositions for which conclusive evidence is lacking, regarded by the believer as of supreme importance. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be constructed from political ideology. Suppose an ideologue believes that a particular economic system will inevitably triumph over all alternatives. Conclusive evidence is lacking. The belief is sustained by an act of will in the face of contrary evidence. The ideologue regards the belief as of supreme importance, organising his entire life around it. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that this is not faith in any religiously significant sense but ideological commitment or political fanaticism. What the background cases reveal — and what the definition fails to require — is that faith in its philosophically and religiously significant sense involves an orientation towards a transcendent or ultimate reality, and that without this dimension the concept of faith collapses into any form of passionate conviction regarding unproven propositions of personal importance.

background_cases: When the Decalogue prohibits murder, the prohibition is understood within the tradition as morally binding not merely because it is useful or rationally defensible but because it is issued by a divine being whose authority over moral matters is supreme. The binding force of the commandment is grounded in the character and authority of the commander.

When the divine command to practise hospitality towards strangers appears across multiple religious traditions, the command's moral force is understood to derive not from the social utility of hospitality but from the divine source of the injunction. The divine being's concern for the vulnerable is taken to be the ground of the obligation.

When the prophetic literature commands justice for the poor and the oppressed, the command's authority rests upon the character of the divine being who issues it: a being whose nature is essentially just and compassionate, so that the command is not an arbitrary decree but an expression of the commander's essential moral nature.
definition: An action A is morally required if and only if (i) A is commanded by a divine being, (ii) the divine being who commands A possesses supreme moral authority, and (iii) the command to perform A is consistent with the established moral teachings of the tradition in which the command is issued.
conditions: A is commanded by a divine being
The divine being who commands A possesses supreme moral authority
The command to perform A is consistent with the established moral teachings of the tradition
counterexample: Suppose a divine being commands, through an authoritative prophet, that a community devote one day in seven to rest and worship. The command is issued by a divine being possessing supreme moral authority. And the command is consistent with the established moral teachings of the tradition. All three conditions are satisfied. Now suppose a member of the community finds herself in a situation where a neighbour is gravely ill on the designated day of rest, and the only way to save the neighbour's life is to undertake arduous physical labour that violates the command. Obedience to the command requires allowing the neighbour to die; disobedience saves a life. All three conditions indicate that rest is morally required, yet moral intuition strongly insists that saving the life takes precedence.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the divine commands in question — prohibitions of murder, injunctions to hospitality, demands for justice — align with the deepest moral intuitions of the community, so that no tension arises between the command and the deliverances of conscience. The definition fails to provide a mechanism for resolving conflicts between divine commands and moral intuition, so that when obedience to a command would produce a morally repugnant outcome, the definition offers no guidance on whether the command or the intuition should yield.

The most widely accepted divine commands in the major religious traditions share a striking feature: they align with the deepest moral convictions of the communities to which they are addressed. When the Decalogue prohibits murder, the prohibition accords with a moral intuition so fundamental that it scarcely requires argument. When hospitality towards strangers is commanded across traditions, the command resonates with a widely shared sense of the dignity of the vulnerable. When the prophetic literature demands justice for the poor, the demand expresses what conscience independently affirms.

These cases invite a general characterisation: an action is morally required when it is commanded by a divine being of supreme moral authority and the command is consistent with the tradition's established moral teachings. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be constructed from the practice of sabbath observance. Suppose a divine being commands that one day in seven be devoted to rest and worship. The command is issued by a being of supreme moral authority and is consistent with the tradition's moral teachings. All three conditions are met. Now suppose a member of the community discovers, on the designated day of rest, that a neighbour is gravely ill and will die unless she undertakes arduous physical labour to carry him to a physician. Obedience to the command requires that she refrain from labour; the neighbour will perish. Disobedience saves a human life. The definition, by making moral requirement depend upon divine command, traditional consistency, and supreme authority, provides no mechanism for resolving the conflict between the command and the deliverance of conscience. What the background cases reveal — and what the definition fails to address — is that the apparent harmony between divine command and moral intuition may be broken by particular cases, and that an adequate account must specify how such conflicts are to be resolved.

background_cases: When Aquinas argued from the existence of motion to the existence of a first unmoved mover, the argument derived its force from the principle that an actually infinite regress of movers is impossible. The conclusion that a first mover exists was reached by demonstrating that the alternative — an infinite chain of dependent movers — leads to an impossibility.

When Leibniz argued from the contingency of the world to the existence of a necessary being, the argument drew its force from the principle of sufficient reason: every contingent fact requires an explanation, and the chain of contingent explanations must terminate in a being whose existence is self-explanatory.

When Anselm argued that a being than which nothing greater can be conceived must exist in reality and not merely in the understanding, the argument drew its force from the claim that existence in reality is a perfection, and that a being lacking this perfection would not be the greatest conceivable.
definition: An argument for the existence of a supreme being is rationally compelling if and only if (i) it proceeds from premises that any rational person would accept upon reflection, (ii) its logical structure is deductively valid, and (iii) its conclusion establishes the existence of a being that is the ultimate explanation of all contingent existence.
conditions: The argument proceeds from premises any rational person would accept upon reflection
Its logical structure is deductively valid
The conclusion establishes the existence of a being that is the ultimate explanation of all contingent existence
counterexample: Suppose a philosopher constructs an argument from the premise that every contingent being requires a cause, through a chain of deductively valid inferences, to the conclusion that there exists a necessary being that is the ultimate explanation of all contingent existence. The premises are such that any rational person would accept them upon reflection, and the logical structure is impeccable. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the argument establishes only that some necessary being exists; it does not establish that this being possesses intelligence, will, or any of the attributes that would make it recognisable as divine. The being could be an impersonal necessary substance or an abstract logical ground. The argument satisfies every condition of the definition yet falls short of establishing the existence of anything that could properly be called a supreme being in the religious sense.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share — and what the definition fails to capture — is that each classical argument does not merely establish the existence of a necessary or ultimate being but attributes to it specific characteristics — unmoved motion, self-explanatory necessity, unsurpassable greatness — that connect the metaphysical conclusion to the theological concept of divinity. A definition that requires only the establishment of an ultimate explanation fails to bridge the gap between a metaphysical ground and a religiously significant supreme being.

The classical arguments for the existence of a supreme being share a structural ambition that merits careful examination. Aquinas argued from motion to an unmoved mover, attributing to the first mover characteristics that connect it to the theological concept of divinity. Leibniz argued from contingency to a necessary being whose self-explanatory nature was meant to ground not merely existence but intelligibility. Anselm argued from conceivability to a being whose greatness surpassed every alternative, connecting metaphysical necessity to divine perfection.

These cases invite a general characterisation: an argument for the existence of a supreme being is rationally compelling when it proceeds from universally acceptable premises, is deductively valid, and concludes to the existence of an ultimate explanation of contingent existence. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be constructed as follows. Suppose a philosopher argues from the premise that every contingent being requires a cause, through impeccable deductive reasoning, to the conclusion that there exists a necessary being that is the ultimate explanation of all contingent existence. The premises are acceptable upon reflection, and the logic is valid. All three conditions are met. Yet the argument establishes only that some necessary being exists; it does not establish that this being possesses intelligence, will, or any attribute that would make it recognisable as divine. It might be an impersonal substance or an abstract logical ground. What the background cases reveal is that each classical argument attributes specific characteristics to its conclusion that bridge the gap between a metaphysical ground and a religiously significant being. A definition that requires only ultimate explanatory status fails to capture this bridging function.

background_cases: When the Gospel accounts describe the multiplication of loaves and fishes, the event's status as miraculous depends upon the assumption that natural processes cannot produce food from nothing. The miracle is identified by contrast with the regular order of nature: what makes the event extraordinary is precisely that it exceeds what the known regularities of nature would lead one to expect.

When hagiographical accounts describe the incorruptibility of a saint's body after death, the event is regarded as miraculous because the normal biological processes of decomposition, which are well understood and highly reliable, have apparently failed to occur. The miracle is identified by the failure of a natural regularity that ordinarily holds without exception.

When accounts of healing at pilgrimage sites describe the sudden remission of diseases diagnosed as incurable by physicians, the miraculous character of the healing depends upon the medical judgement that no natural process could have produced the remission. The identification of the miracle presupposes the reliability of the medical assessment.
definition: An event E is properly identified as miraculous if and only if (i) E is contrary to the established regularities of nature as currently understood, (ii) the violation of natural regularity is attested by credible witnesses, and (iii) no natural explanation of E is available at the time of its occurrence.
conditions: E is contrary to established regularities of nature as currently understood
The violation is attested by credible witnesses
No natural explanation is available at the time of occurrence
counterexample: Suppose a group of credible witnesses in the eighteenth century observes a stone falling from the sky. The event is contrary to the established understanding of nature at the time, for the prevailing scientific opinion holds that stones do not exist in the heavens and cannot fall from there. No natural explanation is available: the phenomenon defies the known regularities of nature. All three conditions are satisfied, and by the proposed definition the event qualifies as miraculous. Yet the stone is a meteorite, and its fall is entirely explicable by natural processes that were not understood at the time. The event was classified as miraculous only because the natural explanation was beyond the reach of contemporary science.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share — and what the definition fails to capture — is that the identification of a miracle requires not merely the absence of a natural explanation at the time of occurrence, but the impossibility in principle of any natural explanation. The background cases implicitly assume that the natural regularities against which the miracle is measured are genuine and complete, so that the violation is not merely apparent. A definition that relies upon the current state of understanding cannot distinguish between genuine violations of natural law and events that are merely unexplained by the science of the day.

The identification of the miraculous in the great religious traditions rests upon a contrast with the regular order of nature. The multiplication of loaves is miraculous because natural processes do not produce food from nothing. The incorruptibility of a saint's body is miraculous because decomposition ordinarily proceeds without exception. The sudden remission of an incurable disease is miraculous because medical science judges the remission to be beyond the power of natural processes. In each case, the miraculous character of the event is identified by its departure from established natural regularity.

These cases invite a general characterisation: an event is miraculous when it is contrary to established natural regularities, attested by credible witnesses, and unexplained by any available natural account. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the history of meteorology. Suppose credible witnesses in the eighteenth century observe a stone falling from the sky. The event is contrary to the established understanding of nature, for the prevailing scientific opinion holds that stones do not inhabit the heavens. No natural explanation is available. All three conditions are satisfied, and the event qualifies as miraculous by the proposed definition. Yet the stone is a meteorite, and its fall is entirely explicable by natural processes that were simply not understood at the time. What the background cases reveal is that the identification of the miraculous requires not merely the current absence of a natural explanation but the impossibility in principle of any such explanation. A definition anchored to the present state of science cannot distinguish genuine miracles from events that are merely beyond contemporary understanding.

background_cases: When Abraham, according to the scriptural account, obeyed the command to sacrifice his son, his faith was characterised not by the certainty of his belief but by his willingness to act despite the anguish and incomprehensibility of the command. The depth of his faith was measured by the magnitude of what he was prepared to sacrifice.

When Pascal argued that the rational person should wager on the existence of God, the argument addressed a situation in which certainty was unavailable and the agent was compelled to act under conditions of radical uncertainty. Faith, on Pascal's account, involved a commitment made in the absence of decisive evidence.

When Kierkegaard described the leap of faith, he characterised it as a transition that reason could not underwrite: the individual must commit without the assurance that rational deliberation could provide. Faith, on this account, was essentially an act performed in the face of objective uncertainty.
definition: A person P exhibits genuine faith if and only if (i) P holds a belief concerning a matter on which conclusive evidence is unavailable, (ii) P acts in accordance with that belief despite the absence of such evidence, and (iii) P's commitment persists through circumstances that might otherwise lead a rational person to abandon the belief.
conditions: P holds a belief on a matter where conclusive evidence is unavailable
P acts in accordance with the belief despite absence of conclusive evidence
P's commitment persists through circumstances that might lead a rational person to abandon it
counterexample: Suppose a man believes, without conclusive evidence, that a particular investment will yield a fortune. He acts on this belief by committing his entire savings to the investment. And his commitment persists through a series of losses that would lead any rational person to withdraw: he interprets each loss as a temporary setback and redoubles his investment. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet no one would describe this man's dogged commitment to a failing investment as an instance of genuine faith in any religious or philosophically significant sense. His persistence is better described as obstinacy or delusion than as faith.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share — and what the definition fails to capture — is that genuine faith is directed towards an object of ultimate or transcendent significance: the divine command, the existence of God, the meaning of human existence. The definition captures the formal structure of faith — belief, action, and persistence under uncertainty — but omits the requirement that the belief concern a matter of transcendent or ultimate import. Without this requirement, any obstinate commitment to an uncertain practical venture satisfies the definition.
passage:
The most profound reflections upon faith in the Western tradition converge upon the theme of commitment under conditions of radical uncertainty. When Abraham obeyed the command to sacrifice his son, his faith was measured by his willingness to act despite the anguish of incomprehension. When Pascal proposed his wager, he addressed a situation in which certainty was unavailable and the agent was compelled to commit. When Kierkegaard described the leap of faith, he characterised it as a transition that reason could not guarantee.

These cases invite a general characterisation: genuine faith involves holding a belief on a matter where conclusive evidence is unavailable, acting upon that belief, and persisting through circumstances that might lead a rational person to abandon it. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from ordinary life. Suppose a man believes, without conclusive evidence, that a particular investment will yield great returns. He acts on this belief by committing his savings. And his commitment persists through a series of losses that would cause any reasonable person to withdraw. All three conditions are met. Yet his dogged commitment is better described as obstinacy than as faith. What the background cases reveal is that genuine faith is directed towards an object of ultimate or transcendent significance: the divine, the meaning of human existence, the ground of moral obligation. The definition captures the formal structure of commitment under uncertainty but omits the requirement that the belief concern a matter of transcendent import. Without this requirement, any stubborn attachment to an uncertain practical venture qualifies as faith.

background_cases: When Leibniz argued that this is the best of all possible worlds, the argument rested on the principle that an omnipotent and perfectly good being would create the best world achievable. The suffering that exists in the actual world, on this account, is a necessary component of the overall optimum: removing any particular evil would make the world as a whole worse.

When Augustine argued that evil is not a positive reality but a privation of good, the argument sought to reconcile the existence of evil with divine perfection by denying that evil has independent ontological status. Suffering, on this view, is the absence of a good that ought to be present, rather than a positive creation of the divine.

When Irenaeus argued that suffering serves the purpose of soul-making, providing the conditions necessary for the development of moral and spiritual virtues, the argument sought to show that evil is instrumentally justified: it exists because without it, the goods of courage, compassion, and perseverance could not be realised.
definition: A response to the existence of suffering is philosophically adequate if and only if (i) it provides a reason that is sufficient to justify the permission of suffering by an omnipotent and perfectly good being, (ii) the reason applies to every type of suffering, including extreme and apparently gratuitous instances, and (iii) the response does not diminish or trivialise the reality of the suffering it seeks to justify.
conditions: It provides a reason sufficient to justify divine permission of suffering
The reason applies to every type of suffering including extreme and apparently gratuitous instances
The response does not diminish or trivialise the reality of suffering
counterexample: Suppose a philosopher argues that all suffering is divinely permitted because it contributes to the overall aesthetic harmony of creation: just as a painting requires dark colours to set off the light, so the cosmos requires suffering to set off the beauty of joy and flourishing. The justification is sufficient in principle, for aesthetic harmony is a genuine good. The reason applies to every type of suffering, including extreme instances, for even the darkest suffering contributes to the cosmic contrast. And the response does not trivialise suffering, for it acknowledges the reality and severity of each instance. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the response treats the suffering of actual persons as a means to an impersonal aesthetic end, subordinating the individual's anguish to a pattern that can be appreciated only from a standpoint that no sufferer could occupy.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share — and what the definition fails to capture — is that each classical response attempts to show that the justification for suffering bears some intelligible relation to the good of the individual who suffers, not merely to an impersonal cosmic pattern. The definition, by requiring only a sufficient reason, universal applicability, and non-trivialisation, fails to demand that the justification respect the dignity and individual significance of each sufferer. A response that treats persons as instruments of an impersonal purpose satisfies every condition while violating a moral intuition that the classical responses take seriously.
passage:
The classical responses to the existence of suffering share an aspiration that the most natural formulations of adequacy fail to articulate. When Leibniz argued that this is the best possible world, he sought to show that the suffering endured by each creature is a necessary component of an overall good that includes the good of the creatures themselves. When Augustine characterised evil as a privation, he aimed to preserve the goodness of creation while acknowledging the genuine deficiency that suffering represents. When Irenaeus argued for soul-making, he connected the justification of suffering to the moral development of the very persons who endure it.

These cases invite a general characterisation: a response to suffering is adequate when it provides a sufficient justifying reason applicable to every type of suffering without trivialising any instance. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be imagined. Suppose a philosopher argues that all suffering is justified because it contributes to the overall aesthetic harmony of creation, as dark colours in a painting set off the light. The justification is in principle sufficient, applies to every type of suffering, and does not trivialise any instance. All three conditions are met. Yet the response treats the suffering of actual persons as a means to an impersonal aesthetic end, subordinating individual anguish to a pattern appreciable only from a standpoint no sufferer could occupy. What the background cases reveal is that each classical response seeks to connect the justification to the good of the individual sufferer, not merely to an impersonal cosmic design. A definition that omits this demand admits responses that satisfy every formal condition while violating the moral intuition that persons are not mere instruments of an impersonal purpose.

definition: The existence of God is established by an argument from design if and only if the natural world exhibits complex order that is best explained by intelligent agency, no natural process is capable of producing such order without guidance, and the intelligent agent responsible must possess attributes traditionally ascribed to God.
conditions: The natural world exhibits complex order best explained by intelligent agency
No natural process is capable of producing such order without guidance
The intelligent agent responsible possesses attributes traditionally ascribed to God
background_cases: The intricate structure of the human eye, with its lens, retina, and optic nerve working in concert, was cited by Paley as exhibiting complex order that is best explained by an intelligent designer rather than by chance.

The precise values of fundamental physical constants, which appear fine-tuned for the existence of life, have been cited as evidence of design, since no known natural process explains why these values fall within the narrow life-permitting range.
The information-bearing properties of DNA, encoding the instructions for biological development, have been presented as analogous to a language or code, suggesting an intelligent author.
counterexample: Consider a case in which one examines the complex order exhibited by snowflakes. Each snowflake displays an intricate hexagonal symmetry of extraordinary beauty and complexity. One might suppose that such order is best explained by intelligent agency. Yet the formation of snowflakes is entirely explicable by the physics of water crystallisation; no guidance is required. The complex order arises from the molecular properties of water interacting with temperature and humidity. This case satisfies the first condition, for the natural world does exhibit complex order, but it decisively fails the second, revealing that complex order can arise from unguided natural processes. The difficulty is that the inference from complex order to intelligent agency cannot be sustained when natural processes demonstrably produce comparable complexity without guidance, and the background cases may have been selected precisely because the relevant natural processes were not yet understood.
abductive_insight: The background cases suggest that complex natural order requires intelligent design because no natural process can produce it unguided. Yet the snowflake case demonstrates that natural processes can produce extraordinary complexity and order without any guidance whatever. The inductive generalisation from cases where the natural mechanism was unknown to the conclusion that no natural mechanism exists confuses present ignorance with principled impossibility. What appeared to require design may simply await a naturalistic explanation.
passage:
The argument from design has been among the most influential considerations in the philosophy of religion, and its appeal rests upon certain paradigmatic cases. Paley famously cited the intricate structure of the human eye as exhibiting a complexity that could only be the work of an intelligent designer, since no natural process seemed capable of producing such an organ by chance. More recently, the precise values of the fundamental physical constants have been adduced as evidence of design, on the ground that no known natural process accounts for why these values fall within the narrow range that permits the existence of life. The information-bearing structure of DNA has been similarly invoked, its resemblance to a code or language suggesting an intelligent author. From these cases, one might inductively generalise that complex natural order is best explained by intelligent agency, that no natural process can produce such order unguided, and that the agent in question must possess the traditional divine attributes.

Yet we should hesitate to accept this generalisation without considering a revealing difficulty. Let us examine the formation of snowflakes. Each snowflake exhibits an intricate hexagonal symmetry of remarkable beauty and complexity, a degree of order that might, on first encounter, seem to demand an intelligent explanation. However, the formation of snowflakes is thoroughly understood in terms of the physics of water crystallisation. The molecular structure of water, interacting with local conditions of temperature and humidity, produces the hexagonal lattice without any guidance or design. Here is complex natural order that demonstrably arises from unguided physical processes. This case reveals that the second condition of our analysis, that no natural process can produce the observed order, cannot be established merely by pointing to complexity. The background cases may have been compelling precisely because the relevant natural mechanisms were not yet understood, and the inference from complexity to design may have rested upon what was, in effect, an argument from ignorance. We are thus led to recognise that inductive generalisation from cases of unexplained complexity to the impossibility of naturalistic explanation confuses our present ignorance with a principled limitation of nature.

definition: Testimony to a miracle is credible if and only if the witnesses are numerous and of good character, the event testified to is publicly observable and not confined to private experience, and no natural explanation for the event has been found.
conditions: The witnesses are numerous and of good character
The event is publicly observable and not confined to private experience
No natural explanation for the event has been found
background_cases: The reported miracle at Lourdes in 1858, where multiple witnesses of good reputation attested to a publicly visible spring appearing at the instruction of an apparition, was taken as credible testimony because the witnesses were numerous, the spring was publicly observable, and no natural explanation was immediately available.

The reported miracle of the sun at Fatima in 1917, witnessed by tens of thousands of persons in an open field, was held to be credible testimony because of the number and diversity of the witnesses, the public nature of the event, and the absence of a ready natural explanation.
counterexample: Suppose that in a remote village, dozens of witnesses of good character report that a statue of a saint wept tears of blood. The event was publicly observable; many persons saw the tears. No natural explanation was initially found. All three conditions are met. However, subsequent investigation reveals that the statue was made of a porous material that had absorbed a reddish liquid during a recent flood, and that changes in temperature caused the liquid to seep from the eyes. The natural explanation was not immediately available but was discovered upon careful examination. The testimony was credible by the stated criteria at the time it was given, yet the event was not miraculous. The analysis provides no mechanism for revisiting credibility assessments when natural explanations are subsequently discovered.
abductive_insight: The background cases suggest that numerous witnesses of good character testifying to a publicly observable event with no known natural explanation constitute credible testimony to a miracle. Yet the weeping statue case reveals that the absence of a natural explanation at the time of testimony does not entail the absence of a natural explanation altogether. Inductive generalisation from cases where no natural explanation was found to the conclusion that none exists conflates the current state of inquiry with a settled metaphysical fact.
passage:
The question of when testimony to a miracle ought to be credited has been debated since at least the eighteenth century. One may observe that certain cases have been taken as paradigmatic of credible miraculous testimony. At Lourdes in 1858, multiple witnesses of acknowledged good character testified to the appearance of a spring at the site of a reported apparition, and no natural explanation for the spring's sudden emergence was forthcoming. At Fatima in 1917, tens of thousands of persons, including sceptics and journalists, reported extraordinary solar phenomena in an open field, and again no natural explanation was readily available. From these cases, one might inductively conclude that testimony to a miracle is credible when the witnesses are numerous and trustworthy, the event is publicly observable, and no natural explanation has been found.

Yet we should hesitate to regard this generalisation as fully adequate. Let us imagine that in a remote village, several dozen persons of good character report that a statue of a saint has wept tears of blood. The event is publicly observable: many persons see the reddish tears upon the statue's face. No natural explanation presents itself, and the local community regards the event as miraculous. All three conditions of the analysis are satisfied. Some months later, however, a careful investigation reveals that the statue is made of a porous material that had absorbed a reddish liquid during a recent flood. Fluctuations in ambient temperature caused the liquid to seep slowly through the porous surface, emerging at the eyes and giving the appearance of bloody tears. The natural explanation was not available at the time of the original testimony but was uncovered by patient inquiry. The testimony was credible by the stated criteria when it was given, yet the event was entirely natural. We are thus led to recognise that the absence of a natural explanation at any given time does not constitute evidence that no such explanation exists. The inductive generalisation from cases where no natural explanation was found conflates the present state of inquiry with a settled conclusion, and the analysis must be supplemented with a recognition that credibility assessments remain provisional and subject to revision.

definition: Religious faith is rational if and only if the believer has considered the available evidence, the evidence does not conclusively refute the belief, and the belief provides a coherent framework for understanding the believer's experience.
conditions: The believer has considered the available evidence
The evidence does not conclusively refute the belief
The belief provides a coherent framework for understanding the believer's experience
background_cases: A Christian who has studied the arguments for and against the existence of a creator, finds the evidence inconclusive rather than refuting, and discovers that her faith provides a coherent framework for understanding her experience of beauty, moral obligation, and purposefulness, is regarded as holding her faith rationally.

A Jewish philosopher who has wrestled with the problem of suffering, concluded that it does not conclusively refute the existence of a providential God, and finds that his tradition provides a coherent framework for understanding communal life and ethical commitment, is regarded as rational in his faith.

A Muslim scholar who has examined the philosophical arguments and the historical evidence, found no conclusive refutation of her beliefs, and experiences her faith as providing a coherent account of the moral and spiritual dimensions of her life, is held to be rational in her belief.
counterexample: Suppose that a man has carefully considered the available evidence regarding the claims of a particular religious movement. The evidence does not conclusively refute the movement's central tenets, and the belief system provides him with a remarkably coherent framework for understanding his experiences, including his sense of purpose, his moral commitments, and his feeling of belonging to a community. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the movement in question employs sophisticated techniques of psychological manipulation, and the coherence the man experiences is a product of systematic isolation from disconfirming information and the social reinforcement of conformity. His belief is rational by the stated criteria, but the process by which he arrived at and maintains his belief is one that systematically undermines the very capacities for rational assessment that the analysis presupposes.
abductive_insight: The background cases suggest that consideration of evidence, absence of conclusive refutation, and experiential coherence are sufficient for the rationality of faith. Yet the case of the manipulative movement reveals that these conditions can be satisfied when the believer's rational capacities have been systematically compromised. Inductive generalisation from cases where the believer's cognitive environment was healthy overlooks the possibility that the conditions of the analysis may be met precisely because the believer has been placed in circumstances designed to ensure that they are met, irrespective of truth.
passage:
The question of whether religious faith can be rational has been much debated. One may observe that certain cases appear to illustrate rational faith. A Christian who has seriously engaged with the arguments of both theists and atheists, who finds the evidence inconclusive rather than decisive against her beliefs, and who discovers that her faith provides a coherent framework for understanding her experience of beauty, moral obligation, and purpose, is widely regarded as holding her faith in a rational manner. A Jewish philosopher who has wrestled deeply with the problem of suffering and found that it does not conclusively refute the existence of a providential God, and who experiences his tradition as providing a coherent account of communal life and ethical commitment, is similarly regarded as rational. A Muslim scholar who has examined the philosophical and historical evidence, found no conclusive refutation, and experienced her faith as coherently illuminating the moral and spiritual dimensions of her life is held to believe rationally. From these cases, one might generalise that faith is rational when the believer has considered the evidence, found no conclusive refutation, and discovered experiential coherence.

Yet we should hesitate to accept this generalisation without noting a disquieting possibility. Let us imagine a person who has carefully considered the available evidence concerning a particular religious movement and found that its central tenets are not conclusively refuted. He experiences his belief as providing a remarkably coherent framework for understanding his life, his sense of purpose, his moral commitments, and his feeling of community. All three conditions are met. However, the movement employs sophisticated techniques of psychological influence, systematically isolating its members from disconfirming information and reinforcing conformity through social pressure. The coherence the man experiences is not the natural result of reflective engagement with reality but the engineered product of a controlled cognitive environment. His faith satisfies the stated criteria of rationality, yet the process by which he arrived at and sustains his belief is one that has systematically undermined the capacities for rational assessment that the analysis presupposes. We are thus led to recognise that the inductive generalisation from cases where the believer's cognitive environment was healthy overlooks the crucial question of whether the conditions of the analysis are met because of genuine rational engagement or because of circumstances designed to manufacture their satisfaction.

definition: A theodicy is successful if and only if it identifies a morally sufficient reason for God to permit suffering, the reason is consistent with the divine attributes of omnipotence and perfect goodness, and the suffering in question is shown to be necessary for the achievement of the identified good.
conditions: It identifies a morally sufficient reason for God to permit suffering
The reason is consistent with divine omnipotence and perfect goodness
The suffering is shown to be necessary for the achievement of the identified good
background_cases: The soul-making theodicy holds that suffering is permitted because it is necessary for the development of virtues such as courage, compassion, and perseverance, and that an omnipotent, perfectly good God permits suffering because the moral development it enables could not be achieved otherwise.

The free-will theodicy holds that God permits suffering because it is a necessary consequence of granting creatures free will, and that a world with free agents who sometimes choose evil is better than a world of mere automata, consistent with divine goodness and omnipotence.
counterexample: Consider a case in which a natural disaster strikes a remote island, killing every inhabitant, including infants who had no capacity for moral development and animals incapable of free choice. No soul-making occurs, for the victims perished without opportunity for moral growth. No free will is exercised, for the disaster is a natural event unrelated to human choice. One searches for a morally sufficient reason consistent with omnipotence and perfect goodness but finds none that does not appeal to inscrutable divine purposes beyond human comprehension. The suffering appears genuinely gratuitous, and the conditions of the analysis cannot be satisfied without retreating to the claim that the reason exists but is simply unknowable, which renders the theodicy unfalsifiable and therefore empty as a philosophical explanation.
abductive_insight: The background cases suggest that morally sufficient reasons for suffering can be identified and shown to be consistent with the divine attributes. Yet the case of natural disaster killing infants and animals reveals a class of suffering for which no morally sufficient reason can be specified without retreating to inscrutability. The inductive generalisation from cases where suffering is plausibly connected to moral development or free will fails when confronted with suffering that bears no relation to either. The theodical strategy succeeds only for a subset of suffering and cannot be generalised to all instances without becoming vacuous.
passage:
The attempt to reconcile the existence of suffering with the existence of an omnipotent and perfectly good God has generated several notable theodicies. The soul-making theodicy maintains that suffering is necessary for the cultivation of moral virtues: courage requires danger, compassion requires the sight of others in pain, and perseverance requires obstacles. An omnipotent God, on this account, permits suffering because the moral growth it enables is of such surpassing value that it could not be achieved by other means. The free-will theodicy, for its part, holds that suffering is a consequence of the exercise of creaturely freedom, and that a world in which agents may freely choose, even to do evil, is of greater worth than a world of automata. From these cases, one might inductively generalise that a theodicy succeeds when it identifies a morally sufficient reason for suffering, demonstrates its consistency with omnipotence and perfect goodness, and shows the suffering to be necessary for the good in question.

Yet we should hesitate to suppose that this generalisation extends to all instances of suffering. Let us consider a natural disaster that devastates a remote island, killing every inhabitant. Among the dead are infants, incapable of moral reasoning and too young for any process of soul-making to have begun, and animals, for whom the concept of moral development has no application. No human free will was exercised in bringing about the catastrophe; it was an event of nature. One searches earnestly for a morally sufficient reason consistent with divine omnipotence and perfect goodness, but every candidate reason presupposes capacities or circumstances that are absent in this case. The only recourse is to appeal to reasons that are real but beyond human comprehension, inscrutable divine purposes that we cannot identify. Yet this retreat to inscrutability renders the theodicy unfalsifiable and, as a philosophical explanation, vacuous. We are thus led to recognise that the inductive generalisation from cases where suffering is plausibly linked to moral growth or free will fails when confronted with suffering that is connected to neither. The theodical strategy succeeds for a restricted class of cases and cannot be extended to a general account without forfeiting its explanatory content.
